These actuators, placed just upstream of the shock formation point, locally ionize the boundary layer. By applying #13 The Other Way Round, we do not push against the shock but pull energy from it, using the plasma to create a virtual, dynamically-contoured surface that encourages a weaker, isentropic compression wave system, delaying separation. The structure is optimized for strength and flexibility, while the aerodynamic shape is managed by the dynamic, non-structural intermediary.
cases.solution: A transonic wing with a structurally optimized, moderately flexible skin. An embedded array of plasma actuators, acting as a fluidic intermediary, dynamically reshapes the effective aerodynamic surface by manipulating the boundary layer's velocity profile, suppressing shock-induced separation and extending the laminar flow region without requiring a rigid, overly thick, and heavy wing box.

cases.problem: A hydroelectric dam operator reports that during spring melt, the reservoir receives a massive influx of floating woody debris that clogs the intake trash racks. Manual raking is dangerous and slow; a permanent floating boom is ineffective because the debris dives under it during high flow. The operator needs a debris management system that works at variable water levels and flow velocities without constant human intervention.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Define contradiction: We want to increase productivity (debris removal rate) (#39), but a fixed system lacks adaptability to changing water levels and debris types (#35). 2) Matrix (39,35) → principles {15, 29, 14, 25, 10, 13, 1, 24, 7, 17}. 3) Cross-domain analogy: Consider how a whale filters krill—baleen plates with a sweeping tongue. 4) Apply #1 Segmentation: break the trash rack into independent, articulated segments that can move individually. 5) Apply #15 Dynamics: make each segment able to change its angle and depth based on flow. 6) Apply #29 Pneumatics and Hydraulics: use the river's own hydraulic force to actuate the segments—no external power. 7) Apply #14 Spheroidality - Curvature: shape each rack bar with a curved leading edge to shed debris sideways, not trap it. 8) Apply #17 Another Dimension: arrange segments in a spiral staircase formation so debris is guided upward and out of the water column. 9) Apply #10 Preliminary Action: pre-position a net downstream during high-debris forecasts. 10) Apply #25 Self-Service: debris accumulation on one segment triggers its rotation, dumping the load onto a conveyor. 11) Apply #13 The Other Way Round: instead of stopping debris at the rack, actively pump water from behind the rack to create a counter-current that pushes debris away. 12) Apply #7 Nested Doll: the conveyor is stored inside the dam structure and extends only when needed. 13) Apply #24 Intermediary: use air bubbles as an intermediary—a bubble curtain lifts submerged debris to the surface before it reaches the rack. 14) Synthesize: A self-cleaning intake system with segmented, curved racks that rotate using hydraulic force, a bubble curtain for pre-lifting, and a nested conveyor that deploys on demand.
cases.solution: A self-powered, adaptive debris exclusion system: segmented, curved trash rack elements are hinged and counterweighted to rotate when debris load exceeds a threshold, dumping debris onto a retractable screw conveyor nested in the dam face. A bubble curtain generated by a Venturi aerator using intake flow lifts submerged debris to the surface upstream. The system operates autonomously, handles 10X the debris volume of a fixed rack, and requires no external power.

cases.problem: So, picture this: we've got these giant CNC milling machines on the shop floor, right? And the coolant mist they kick up is just everywhere. It's a slippery, sticky fog that coats the floor, the walkways, even the overhead crane rails. Our guys are constantly slipping, and we're spending a fortune on degreaser and rags just to keep the place OSHA-compliant. We tried putting up full plastic strip curtains around each machine, but then the forklift drivers can't see to load raw stock, and it gets so hot inside the enclosure that the machine's electronics start throwing thermal errors. How do we catch the mist without turning the factory into a maze of hot, blind boxes?

contradiction_pair.improving_parameter: Harmful side effects of the object (#30)

cases.reasoning_chain: We're dealing with a classic trade-off. We want to reduce the harmful mist (#30), but full physical enclosures trap heat and worsen the temperature (#17). Looking at the matrix for (30,17), we get principles 2, 35, 30. Let's walk through the thinking. First, #2 Taking Out: we don't need to enclose the whole machine, just separate the mist from the air and the operator. What if we extract the mist right at the source? Second, #35 Parameter Changes: instead of a physical barrier, change the physical state of the mist itself—make it heavier so it falls out of the air quickly. Third, #30 Flexible Shells and Thin Films: we can create a dynamic, invisible 'curtain' of air that contains the mist without trapping heat. So, the solution isn't a solid wall; it's a high-velocity air knife around the machine's work envelope that creates an aerodynamic barrier, combined with a chilled ceiling panel directly above. The cold surface condenses the oil mist into droplets that drip back into the machine's sump, while the air curtain keeps the rest of the shop clean and allows free movement and visibility.
cases.solution: Install a perimeter air-knife system that creates a downward high-velocity air curtain around the CNC machining zone. Directly above the machine, mount a chilled water panel at 10°C. The air curtain contains the mist, which rises, condenses on the cold panel, and drips back into the coolant tank. The shop floor remains mist-free, visibility is unobstructed, and the machine electronics stay within their normal operating temperature range.

cases.problem: A high-speed beverage bottling line (24,000 bottles/hour) requires label adhesive to cure instantly for accurate placement, but this rapid curing generates volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions exceeding regulatory limits (current: 3.2 g/m², limit: 0.5 g/m²).
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Problem: Fast adhesive curing requires solvent flash-off, causing VOC emissions. 2) Contradiction: Improve Speed (curing time), worsen Harmful side effects (emissions). 3) Matrix (9,31) → {28, 35, 40}. 4) Apply #28 Mechanics substitution: Replace solvent-based adhesive with UV-curable adhesive; curing triggered by light, not evaporation. 5) Apply #35 Parameter changes: Modify adhesive viscosity via temperature control to ensure instant tack before UV exposure. 6) Apply #40 Composite materials: Use a dual-layer label with a micro-encapsulated activator that releases upon pressure, eliminating open solvent. 7) Result: Instant cure, zero VOC.
cases.solution: UV-curable hot-melt pressure-sensitive adhesive with integrated micro-encapsulated photoinitiator. Label applied with heated roller (120°C) for immediate tack, then passes under UV LED array (395 nm, 4 W/cm²) for 0.2 s cure. VOC emissions reduced to <0.1 g/m², line speed maintained.

cases.problem: Transport containers for donated whole blood must maintain a uniform 2–6 °C internal environment during a 72‑hour cross‑desert delivery. Existing passive coolers exhibit a 4 °C gradient between top and bottom, causing plasma separation and hemolysis in 8% of units. Specification: internal ΔT ≤ 1.0 °C at any point, no external power, payload 40 blood bags.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The rectangular cooler shape promotes stagnant cold-air pockets and thermal stratification. 2) We want improved Temperature (#17) uniformity, but the fixed rectangular Shape (#12) worsens convection. 3) Matrix (17,12) → {14, 35, 40, 4}. 4) Apply #14 Spheroidality: redesign the inner payload cavity as a vertical cylinder with domed ends to promote natural convection loops. 5) Apply #35 Parameter Changes: replace ice bricks with a phase-change material (PCM) that melts at 3 °C, eliminating cold spots near the walls. 6) Apply #40 Composite Materials: line the cavity with a thin aluminum-PCM-graphite laminate that conducts heat laterally, smoothing gradients. 7) The spherical convection pattern and distributed PCM maintain all blood bags within 0.8 °C.
cases.solution: A cylindrical passive cooler with domed top and bottom, lined with a graphite‑PCM‑aluminum composite that melts at 3 °C. The curvature induces a toroidal convection cell, reducing internal ΔT to 0.8 °C over 72 h without external power.

cases.problem: Customer complaint: "Your insulin pen cartridges arrive with the rubber piston swollen and stuck. I can't dial a dose — the pen clicks but the piston doesn't move. It's like the insulin has eaten the rubber. Happens every summer. I'm switching to vials." Root cause: the bromobutyl rubber piston absorbs the phenolic preservative (m‑cresol) from the insulin formulation at temperatures >30 °C, swelling by 3–5% and increasing static friction beyond the pen's 15 N drive limit.
cases.reasoning_chain: Step 1: Identify the conflicting requirements. We must prevent m‑cresol from swelling the piston (improve chemical stability #13), but any change to the piston material or coating must not make the cartridge impossible to assemble on existing high‑speed lines (worsen manufacturability #32). Step 2: Consult the contradiction matrix. (13,32) → 40, 24, 10, 30, 35. Step 3: Consider a cross‑domain analogy — marine anti‑fouling coatings use a sacrificial layer that absorbs the aggressive agent before it reaches the substrate. Step 4: Apply #40 Composite Materials: create a two‑layer piston. The core is standard bromobutyl for elasticity; the skin is a 50 µm layer of PTFE‑expanded fluoropolymer, inert to m‑cresol. Step 5: But a PTFE skin alone would delaminate under the injection molding process. Apply #24 Intermediary: introduce a tie‑layer of maleic‑anhydride‑grafted EPDM between the core and the PTFE skin to covalently bond them during vulcanization. Step 6: Now the piston is chemically stable, but the PTFE surface is too slippery — the pen drive screw can't grip it. Apply #10 Preliminary Action: mold a hexagonal socket into the piston's rear face during forming. The pen's drive rod now mechanically keys into the socket rather than relying on friction. Step 7: The new piston design requires a two‑shot molding process, which is slower. Apply #35 Parameter Changes: instead of two‑shot molding, use a co‑extrusion process to produce a continuous rod of core‑skin‑tie‑layer, then slice pistons off with a hot knife. This runs at 800 pistons/min, matching the original line speed. Step 8: Finally, apply #30 Flexible Shells to the packaging: enclose each cartridge in a metallized PET foil pouch with a silica gel desiccant, heat‑sealed, to prevent any humidity‑accelerated swelling during summer transport. Step 9: The solution eliminates swelling, maintains line speed, and adds a mechanical drive interface.
cases.solution: A tri‑layer piston produced by co‑extrusion: bromobutyl core, maleic‑anhydride‑grafted EPDM tie‑layer, and a 50 µm PTFE skin. The rear face includes a molded hexagonal socket for mechanical drive engagement. Cartridges are individually sealed in foil pouches with desiccant. Piston swelling <0.2% after 24 months at 40 °C; assembly speed 800 pistons/min.

cases.problem: Design a wind turbine blade that achieves maximum aerodynamic lift (high power coefficient) while minimizing structural mass to reduce tower-top weight, yet lighter blades are prone to flutter instability at high tip-speed ratios.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Increasing blade stiffness (strength) to resist flutter typically requires adding mass (worsening weight). 2) Identify contradiction: improve #14 Strength, worsen #1 Weight of moving object. 3) Matrix lookup (14,1) yields {40, 26, 27, 1}. 4) Apply #40 Composite Materials: replace solid fiberglass with carbon-fiber-reinforced polymer (CFRP) spar cap, providing 3× specific stiffness. 5) Apply #4 Asymmetry: shift the spar cap from the blade centerline toward the suction side, creating an asymmetric cross-section that increases torsional stiffness without adding mass. 6) Apply #3 Local Quality: use a balsa-wood core in the trailing edge and CFRP only in the highly loaded spar region, optimizing stiffness distribution. 7) Result: 25% lighter blade with 15% higher flutter margin; validated via aeroelastic simulations at DTU Wind Energy.
cases.solution: Asymmetric CFRP spar cap with balsa-core sandwich construction: unidirectional carbon fiber pultruded plank bonded to the suction side of a glass-fiber shell, with end-grain balsa core in the trailing edge. Reduces blade mass by 25% while increasing first torsional frequency by 18%, eliminating flutter up to tip-speed ratio 9.5.

cases.problem: Concentrated solar power (CSP) plants using molten salt (NaNO3/KNO3) as a heat transfer and storage fluid are limited to a maximum operating temperature of 565°C due to salt decomposition. To achieve higher thermodynamic efficiency (>50%), we need a working fluid stable at >700°C, but all known single-phase fluids either decompose (salts), corrode containment (liquid metals), or have high vapor pressure (supercritical CO2).
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) We want to increase the operating temperature (improve #17) but the fluid's chemical stability degrades (worsen #13). 2) Matrix (17,13) suggests principles {35, 21, 2, 28}. 3) Let's cross-domain analogize to a 'heat battery' concept used in steelmaking. 4) Apply #35 Parameter Changes: instead of a homogeneous liquid, change the state of matter – use solid particles as the primary heat carrier. 5) Apply #5 Merging: merge the functions of heat transfer and heat storage by using the same solid particles. 6) Apply #32 Color Changes: not literally color, but use the optical properties – make the particles dark (high absorptivity) so they can be directly heated by concentrated sunlight in a falling-particle receiver. 7) Apply #34 Discarding and Recovering: the particles fall through the receiver, heat up, and are stored in a hot silo; they are 'discarded' from the receiver but 'recovered' for heat exchange. 8) Apply #22 Blessing in Disguise: the inherent instability of molten salt at high temperature is a blessing if we abandon fluids entirely. 9) Apply #31 Porous Materials: use sintered bauxite (ceramic) particles, which are inherently porous and stable to 1000°C. 10) Apply #18 Mechanical Vibration: to discharge heat, vibrate the hot particle bed in a fluidized-bed heat exchanger, enhancing heat transfer to a working gas. 11) Apply #24 Intermediary: the particles act as an intermediary between concentrated sunlight and a supercritical CO2 Brayton cycle. 12) Apply #15 Dynamics: the particle flow rate can be dynamically adjusted to match solar variability. 13) Apply #40 Composite Materials: coat the particles with a high-emissivity layer to improve radiative heating. 14) Result: a particle-based CSP system achieves 700°C+ operation, enabling >50% thermal-to-electric efficiency with sCO2 turbines, demonstrated at Sandia's falling-particle receiver test facility.
cases.solution: Falling-particle receiver with sintered bauxite particles (Carbo HSP 40/70, 0.3-0.6 mm) directly irradiated by a 1 MWth heliostat field. Hot particles at 775°C are stored in a refractory-lined silo and then fed to a fluidized-bed heat exchanger where they heat supercritical CO2 to 700°C for a Brayton cycle. Particle attrition is managed by a continuous particle lift and screening system. Demonstrated 90% thermal efficiency in the receiver and 95% particle-to-sCO2 heat exchanger effectiveness at Sandia National Laboratories (2022).

cases.problem: Achieving high energy density in lithium-sulfur (Li-S) batteries is hindered by the polysulfide shuttle effect, where soluble intermediate species migrate to the anode and cause irreversible capacity loss. Physical confinement of sulfur in porous carbon hosts increases cycle life but reduces the sulfur loading below 2 mg/cm², negating the gravimetric advantage over Li-ion. A trade-off exists between specific capacity (mAh/g) and cycle stability.
cases.reasoning_chain: This study addresses the contradiction between improving specific capacity (#39 Productivity) and suppressing the harmful polysulfide shuttle (#30 Harmful factors) in Li-S cells. Conventional physical confinement strategies (mesoporous carbon) degrade productivity by limiting sulfur loading. We propose a paradigm shift from physical to chemical confinement. Principle #28 Mechanics Substitution is applied by replacing the mechanical barrier of carbon pores with an electrostatic field: a thin, ion-selective membrane of sulfonated tetrafluoroethylene (Nafion) is coated on the separator. This membrane allows Li⁺ transport but repels negatively charged polysulfide anions (Sn²⁻) via Donnan exclusion. To further enhance capacity, Principle #35 Parameter Changes is invoked to alter the electrolyte composition—shifting from a high-dissolution ether solvent (DOL/DME) to a sparingly solvating hydrofluoroether, which promotes a quasi-solid-state discharge mechanism, forming Li₂S directly on the cathode without long-chain polysulfide intermediates. Finally, Principle #24 Intermediary introduces a redox mediator (lithium iodide) in the electrolyte to catalyze the conversion of solid Li₂S back to sulfur on charge, reducing overpotential and enabling high areal loadings of 5 mg/cm². The resulting cell achieves an initial specific capacity of 1350 mAh/g at 0.2C with 85% capacity retention after 300 cycles, demonstrating that chemical and electrochemical strategies can decouple capacity and stability.
cases.solution: A Li-S cell employing a Nafion-coated Celgard separator, a catholyte of 1 M LiTFSI in TTE/HFE (1:1 vol) with 0.1 M LiI additive, and a sulfur-infused Ketjenblack cathode (5 mg/cm² sulfur loading). The cation-selective membrane blocks polysulfide crossover, the sparingly solvating electrolyte enforces a solid-phase sulfur redox pathway, and the LiI mediator facilitates Li₂S oxidation, yielding 1350 mAh/g initial capacity and 85% retention over 300 cycles at 0.2C.

cases.problem: Our field kitchen trailer needs to cook 500 hot meals per hour but the diesel generator exhaust is so loud (95 dB) that troops can't hear commands within 50 meters, and the infrared signature makes it a target at night.
cases.reasoning_chain: We had a field kitchen that was basically a roaring diesel-powered stove. The more meals we pushed out, the louder the generator screamed and the hotter the exhaust glowed. The contradiction was clear: productivity up, but noise and IR signature went through the roof. So we asked, what if the noise and heat weren't even there? Looking at the matrix for improving #39 and worsening #31 gave us #35, #39, #2, and #28. We used #2 Taking out: separate the noisy generator from the cooking unit entirely, tethering it with a long power cable so it can be hidden in a dugout. Then #35 Parameter changes: switch the cooking method from open-flame diesel burners to induction hobs powered by the remote generator, eliminating combustion noise and local exhaust. Finally, #39 Inert atmosphere: we encase the remote generator in a nitrogen-flooded, soundproofed container to suppress combustion noise and cool the exhaust gases before they exit through a diffused, ground-level vent. No more screaming diesel right next to the cooks.
cases.solution: A split-unit field kitchen: the cooking trailer uses silent induction heating, powered by a remote generator placed up to 100 m away. The generator is housed in a nitrogen-inerted, acoustically sealed container that reduces noise to 60 dB at 10 m and cools exhaust to below 150°C, eliminating the IR hotspot.

cases.problem: A temporary military runway mat must provide a load-bearing surface for C-17 cargo aircraft (maximum tire pressure 1.5 MPa) over soft soil, yet be transported in standard ISO containers and installed by a platoon of engineers in under 4 hours for a 500 m runway.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Identify the core conflict: The mat must transmit high aircraft loads (high force) to weak soil, but a single rigid slab would be unadaptable to uneven terrain and impossible to transport in containers. 2) Map to TRIZ parameters: Improve #10 Force (bearing capacity), but this worsens #35 Adaptability (inability to conform to ground contours and fit modular transport). 3) Matrix query (10,35) yields principles {1, 17, 40, 33}. 4) Analyze principle #1 Segmentation: Divide the monolithic surface into many small, interlocking panels. 5) This mirrors how an armadillo's segmented shell provides both strength and flexibility—a cross-domain analogy from biology. 6) Apply #17 Another dimension: Instead of flat panels, give each panel a deep, egg-crate-like cellular structure that spreads load in 3D to the soil. 7) Apply #40 Composite materials: Fabricate panels from a glass-fiber-reinforced polymer with a high strength-to-weight ratio. 8) Apply #33 Homogeneity: Make the panels and their connectors from the same composite material to eliminate galvanic corrosion and differential wear. 9) The segmented, 3D-cellular, composite panels can be nested for transport and laid by hand over minor surface irregularities, adapting to the ground. 10) The deep cells fill with soil, using the soil's own mass to resist panel uplift from jet blast. 11) Result: high force capacity through geometry and material, with high adaptability through segmentation.
cases.solution: A runway mat system composed of 1 m × 1 m interlocking, deep-cell composite panels (GFRP). The panels' 15 cm deep cellular structure spreads 1.5 MPa tire pressure to a safe 200 kPa on CBR 6 soil. Panels nest for containerized transport, and a platoon can hand-place a 500 m runway in 3.5 hours. The soil-filled cells prevent jet-blast uplift.

cases.problem: The fuel-cell stack's bipolar plates are stamped from 0.1 mm 316L stainless steel. After 5,000 hours of operation, the cathode side shows severe pitting corrosion due to the 0.6 V (vs. SHE) potential and pH 3 environment, releasing Fe³⁺ ions that poison the membrane. Using a thicker plate (0.2 mm) reduces corrosion but increases stack mass by 40% and material cost by 35%. How can we maintain the low mass and cost of the thin plate while achieving the corrosion resistance of the thick plate?
cases.reasoning_chain: Q: What exactly are we trying to preserve? A: The thin plate's low mass and cost. Q: And what are we trying to prevent? A: The loss of metal ions into the membrane. Q: Why does the thick plate resist corrosion better? A: It has more sacrificial material, but also the cold-worked surface from stamping is more corrosion-prone. Q: What if we didn't try to make the whole plate corrosion-resistant, but only the parts that actually corrode? A: The cathode flow-field lands are where pitting starts because of water droplet stagnation. Q: Could we copy the surface of a corrosion-resistant material onto the thin steel? A: That's principle #26 Copying. Instead of bulk alloying, we could deposit a thin, cheap, sacrificial coating that mimics the electrochemical behavior of a noble metal. Q: But coatings can crack during stamping. A: Principle #35 Parameter Changes: change the process sequence. Stamp the plates first, then apply the coating. Q: And principle #3 Local Quality: only coat the cathode-side lands, not the entire plate. Use a precision inkjet to deposit a 5 µm carbon-polymer composite only on the land areas. The carbon shifts the local potential below the pitting threshold, and the polymer seals micro-cracks. The coating costs $0.02 per plate and adds 0.5 g mass.
cases.solution: Selective coating process: 0.1 mm 316L plates stamped, then cathode lands coated with 5 µm carbon-black/PVDF (80/20) ink via piezoelectric inkjet (1200 dpi). Coating reduces Fe³⁺ release from 12 ppm to <0.1 ppm after 5,000 h. Stack mass increase <0.3%, cost increase 1.2%.

cases.problem: Proton-exchange membrane fuel cells require humidification of the inlet air to maintain membrane conductivity above 0.1 S/cm. External membrane humidifiers add 15 kg and 8 L of volume to the balance-of-plant, while also consuming 1.2 kW of parasitic power for heating. Self-humidifying membranes using Pt/SiO2 catalyst particles embedded in the membrane reduce external component count but suffer from hydrogen peroxide radical formation that degrades the membrane chemically, reducing lifetime from 5,000 h to 2,000 h. A solution is sought that eliminates the external humidifier mass and volume while achieving the 5,000 h durability target.
cases.reasoning_chain: We examine the trade-off between eliminating humidifier mass and maintaining membrane durability. The external humidifier represents a significant mass penalty (15 kg) that reduces vehicle efficiency. Removing it via self-humidification introduces a chemical degradation pathway where Pt-catalyzed H2 and O2 crossover generates *OH radicals that attack the ionomer. The contradiction matrix maps improving parameter #1 (weight of moving object) against worsening parameter #15 (durability) to principles {24, 31, 5, 40, 35}. Principle #24 (Intermediary) suggests introducing a sacrificial layer that intercepts radicals before they reach the membrane. Instead of Pt/SiO2 particles scattered throughout the membrane, we can place a thin, porous CeO2/ZrO2 interlayer (5 µm) between the membrane and each catalyst layer. This interlayer acts as a radical quencher: Ce³⁺/Ce⁴⁺ redox cycle neutralizes *OH and *OOH radicals without interfering with proton transport. Principle #31 (Porous Materials) is applied to structure the interlayer with aligned 20 nm pores via a block-copolymer template, ensuring proton conductivity of 0.08 S/cm. Principle #5 (Merging) integrates the gas-diffusion layer's microporous layer with this interlayer, so the same structure both manages water and quenches radicals. The resulting membrane-electrode assembly shows <5 µV/h degradation rate over 5,000 h, equivalent to the externally humidified baseline, while eliminating the 15 kg humidifier. A trade-off remains: the CeO2 interlayer increases MEA cost by 12%, but this is offset by the removal of the $400 humidifier unit.
cases.solution: MEA with integrated CeO2/ZrO2 radical-quenching interlayer: 5 µm thick, 20 nm aligned mesopores (PS-b-PEO template), Ce:Zr=4:1 atomic ratio. Spray-coated between 15 µm PFSA membrane and Pt/C catalyst layer (0.4 mg/cm²). Degradation rate 4.8 µV/h over 5,000 h AST (DOE protocol). System mass reduction: 15 kg. Cost delta: -$320 per stack (MEA +$48, humidifier -$368).

cases.problem: BUG REPORT: Wet scrubber unit WS-09 for flue gas desulfurization. Limestone slurry recirculation pump (P-401) suffers severe cavitation damage. Impeller replacement required every 4 months. Root cause: pump is located 2 meters above the slurry sump, NPSH available is only 1.8 m at operating temperature (60°C), while NPSH required is 3.2 m. Raising the pump is structurally impossible; lowering it requires a $500k pit excavation. Slurry is abrasive with 15% solids. Current fix: throttling discharge valve to reduce flow from 1200 m³/h to 800 m³/h, but this reduces SO2 removal efficiency to 88% (permit requires 95%).
cases.reasoning_chain: Q: Why can't we just fix the cavitation by increasing the suction pressure? A: Because the pump's elevation relative to the sump is fixed—that's the core physical constraint. Q: So we need to increase the NPSH at the pump inlet without moving the pump or the sump. What if we changed the fluid's properties right before it enters the pump? A: That's interesting. The NPSH required is a function of the fluid's vapor pressure. Q: How could we lower the vapor pressure locally? We can't cool the whole sump. A: What about injecting a small, high-pressure stream of cooled, degassed slurry back into the pump suction? That's applying #29 Pneumatics and Hydraulics. But that requires a booster pump, adding complexity. Q: What if we think about the impeller itself? The damage is from collapsing vapor bubbles. Can we make the bubbles collapse harmlessly? A: Yes, by changing the surface geometry. Apply #14 Spheroidality - Curvature. Instead of sharp leading edges on the impeller vanes, we use a bulbous, spherical leading edge profile. This geometry guides the bubble collapse away from the metal surface, into the fluid stream. Q: But the pump is still cavitating, just less destructively. Can we eliminate the condition entirely? A: Let's look at the fundamental issue: the liquid column is pulling away from the pump due to gravity. Apply #12 Equipotentiality. Instead of fighting gravity, we work with it. Install a vacuum-rated holding tank right at the pump suction, level with the sump. The pump draws from this tank. A separate, low-head, large-diameter screw pump (insensitive to abrasion and low NPSH) lifts the slurry from the sump to this tank. The screw pump operates at a low speed, submerged, eliminating its own cavitation risk. The main recirculation pump now operates with a flooded suction, permanently solving the NPSH deficit. The screw pump is a simple, low-cost addition compared to excavating a new pit.
cases.solution: A two-stage pumping arrangement. A low-speed, submerged screw pump lifts slurry 2 meters into a small vacuum-break tank at grade level. The main high-pressure recirculation pump draws from this tank with a flooded suction, providing >5 m NPSH margin. Impeller life is extended to 3+ years with no derating of scrubber flow.

cases.problem: Abstract: The removal of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from contaminated groundwater via air stripping is a mature technology, yet its application in cold climates is constrained by a sharp trade-off. Henry's law dictates that removal efficiency increases at higher temperatures, while heating large groundwater flows incurs prohibitive energy costs. A packed-tower air stripper operating year-round in a northern climate sees its removal efficiency for trichloroethylene (TCE) drop from 99.9% in summer (20°C water) to 95% in winter (5°C water), failing the 5 µg/L discharge limit. This study addresses the inherent contradiction between treatment efficiency and energy input.

contradiction_pair.worsening_parameter: Energy consumption of the moving object (#19)

cases.reasoning_chain: The fundamental challenge is to increase the effective treatment temperature (improve #17) without proportionally increasing the energy required to heat the bulk water (worsening #19). The matrix for this contradiction suggests principles #36, #21, and #17. We first apply #36 Phase Transitions. Instead of heating the water, we use the latent heat of the air. In winter, the ambient air is cold and has very low absolute humidity. We can pre-heat and humidify the stripping air using a low-grade waste heat source (e.g., compressor cooling water at 30°C). This warm, moist air provides a two-fold benefit: it heats the water slightly via direct contact, but more importantly, the high humidity suppresses evaporative cooling of the water in the tower, preventing the water temperature from dropping further. This is a phase-change energy transfer that decouples air heating from water heating. Next, we apply #21 Skipping: We do not try to achieve the full 99.9% removal in a single pass during winter. Instead, we accept a 95% removal in the primary tower and then 'skip' the remaining contamination to a much smaller, insulated polishing unit. Finally, we apply #17 Another Dimension: The polishing unit is not a conventional stripper. It is a membrane contactor operating in a liquid-ring vacuum pump configuration. The vacuum pump creates a low-pressure headspace on the permeate side of a hollow-fiber membrane. The TCE partitions into the gas phase at a much higher rate due to the vacuum, even at 5°C. The vacuum pump's seal water is the same groundwater, which is recirculated. The heat from the pump's inefficiency (which is 'wasted' energy) warms this seal water to ~35°C, creating a localized hot zone for high-efficiency TCE removal in the membrane module. The treated seal water is then blended back. This approach acknowledges the trade-off, using a cascade of phase transitions and a spatial skip to a third dimension (vacuum), thereby confining the energy penalty to a minuscule side-stream.
cases.solution: A two-stage winter operation mode. Stage 1: a packed tower using waste-heat-humidified air to maintain water temperature and achieve 95% removal. Stage 2: a vacuum membrane contactor integrated with a liquid-ring pump, which uses its own waste heat to locally warm a small side-stream for polishing removal to >99.9%. Overall system energy use increases by only 15% in winter while maintaining compliance.

cases.problem: A ship's propeller must operate at high RPM for speed, but cavitation at high RPM erodes blades and reduces efficiency. The fixed-pitch propeller designed for high-speed cavitates severely at full power.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Higher RPM improves speed but increases cavitation. 2) Improve #9 (speed), worsen #31 (harmful factors from cavitation). 3) Matrix (9,31) → {2, 24, 35, 21}. 4) Apply #15 Dynamics: use controllable-pitch propeller to adjust blade angle based on speed and load. 5) Apply #35 Parameter Changes: optimize blade cross-section to delay cavitation onset. 6) Apply #40 Composite Materials: use carbon-fiber-reinforced polymer blades with cavitation-resistant coating. 7) Result: propeller that maintains high efficiency across speed range with reduced cavitation damage.
cases.solution: Controllable-pitch propeller with skewed composite blades and cavitation-resistant coating, dynamically adjusted by engine management system to optimize pitch for current speed and load.

cases.problem: User complaint: 'Every time we dock the ferry, the rubber fenders squeal like a banshee and wear out in months. We've tried thicker fenders, but then the impact force gets transferred to the hull and cracks the plating. Can't you make something that lasts and doesn't shake the ship apart?'
cases.reasoning_chain:
Q: Why can't we just use a super-strong, solid rubber fender?
A: Because while it would resist wear, it wouldn't absorb energy effectively. The impact force would spike and transmit directly to the hull, causing damage. So we improve durability (#15) but worsen the force transmitted (#10).
Q: What if the fender didn't just compress but also twisted or sheared?
A: That's an idea—using #14 Spheroidality, we could shape the fender as a series of curved lobes that roll and deform, converting impact into rotational and shear motion, not just compression.
Q: But wouldn't that still wear out the surface quickly from all that rubbing?
A: Perhaps, but what if we apply #19 Periodic Action? Instead of a continuous surface, the fender could have a segmented or treaded pattern that engages intermittently, spreading wear. And #31 Porous Materials: the core could be a closed-cell foam that compresses with a progressive rate, absorbing energy without a hard bottoming-out force.
Q: So the outer skin is tough for wear, but the inner core is soft and porous?
A: Exactly—a composite fender with a durable curved outer shell and a porous energy-absorbing core that progressively collapses, reducing peak force on the hull.
cases.solution: A marine fender with a segmented, lobed outer skin made of abrasion-resistant polyurethane and a core of progressive-rate closed-cell foam. The lobes roll and shear during impact, distributing force over time and reducing peak load on the hull by 40% while lasting 3x longer than solid rubber.

cases.problem: A 5-meter-tall cellular base station monopole must be installed on a historic building rooftop. The structural engineer specifies a maximum dead load of 800 kg, but standard concrete counterweight blocks for wind stability require 1200 kg of mass. The site cannot be penetrated for anchor bolts.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Problem: Reducing foundation mass (improving #2) reduces overturning resistance, worsening stability (#13). 2) Apply #1 Segmentation: instead of one monolithic counterweight, break it into multiple smaller interconnected segments. 3) Apply #30 Flexible Shells and Thin Films: encase each segment in a tensioned fabric membrane that distributes load only when wind uplift occurs. 4) Apply #19 Periodic Action: use a tuned mass damper that activates only during gust peaks, providing dynamic stabilization without static mass. 5) Combined effect: total static mass is reduced to 750 kg, but segmented tensioned membranes and periodic damping maintain stability.
cases.solution: Replace monolithic counterweight with segmented, membrane-tensioned ballast pods and a tuned mass damper that engages only during wind gusts, achieving stability at 750 kg static load.

cases.problem: A suspension bridge's main cables are corroding internally despite external protective wire wrapping. The current inspection method requires unwrapping the outer wire, which is destructive, time-consuming, and can only sample a few locations. The bridge owner demands full-length, non-destructive condition assessment of the inner high-strength steel wires without removing the outer wrapping.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The core problem: We want to know the condition inside a multi-layer wire rope (improve ease of repair #34 by enabling targeted maintenance) but accessing it mechanically is complex (#36). 2) Cross-domain analogy: Think of a doctor diagnosing a bone fracture through skin without cutting. They use X-rays (Mechanics Substitution #28). 3) Apply #28: Replace mechanical unwrapping with an electromagnetic or acoustic field. 4) But a simple X-ray of a dense steel bundle is useless. 5) Apply #18 Mechanical Vibration: Use magnetostrictive guided wave ultrasonics. But how to get the signal in and out through the outer wrapping? 6) Apply #10 Preliminary Action: Embed a thin, magnetostrictive strip (#24 Intermediary) inside the cable during its original fabrication. This strip is a sensor. 7) Apply #25 Self-Service: The corroding wires themselves generate magnetic flux leakage as they lose cross-section. The embedded strip can be designed to detect this leakage. 8) Apply #26 Copying: Instead of measuring the wire directly, we measure an optical copy of the magnetic field. Integrate a fiber optic cable alongside the magnetostrictive strip that uses Fiber Bragg Gratings to detect strain from magnetostriction. 9) Apply #35 Parameter Changes: Change the magnetic permeability of the sensing strip by using a material with a giant magneto-impedance effect. 10) Apply #31 Porous Materials: The outer wrapping could be made from a porous, non-metallic composite that allows magnetic fields to pass through without unwrapping. 11) Apply #22 Blessing in Disguise: The corrosion itself generates a small electrical potential (galvanic corrosion), which we can use to power a passive RFID sensor tag (#25 Self-Service) embedded in the cable. 12) Apply #17 Another Dimension: Use the cable's longitudinal axis as a waveguide, sending a pulse from one end and analyzing reflections from internal discontinuities. The embedded sensor network provides a full 3D health map without any destructive disassembly.
cases.solution: Embed a magnetostrictive sensing strip and fiber optic cable with Fiber Bragg Gratings inside the cable during fabrication. Interrogate the strip with a guided wave pulse from the cable end, while the fiber detects magnetostriction strains and passive RFID tags powered by galvanic corrosion potentials transmit localized data through a porous non-metallic outer wrapping.

cases.problem: A waste-to-energy plant requires a furnace capable of burning municipal solid waste with high moisture content (up to 60%) while maintaining a stable combustion temperature of at least 850°C to destroy dioxins, without excessive auxiliary fuel consumption.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) High moisture waste robs heat through evaporation, lowering temperature. 2) We want high temperature (improve) but moisture loss (worsening) kills it. 3) Apply #22 Blessing in Disguise: the moisture itself becomes a reactant. 4) Use #36 Phase Transitions: flash evaporate water into superheated steam. 5) Inject this steam into a secondary gasification zone. 6) Apply #25 Self-Service: the steam reacts with char to produce H2 and CO, which burn and release heat back to the primary chamber. 7) The waste's own water becomes fuel.
cases.solution: Two-stage gasification-combustion furnace where wet waste is dried by radiant heat in a primary chamber, the released steam is drawn into a secondary char bed to drive the water-gas shift reaction (C + H2O → CO + H2), and the resulting syngas is combusted above the bed to maintain the required 850°C without auxiliary fuel.

cases.problem: Achieving high-efficiency thermoelectric conversion in automotive waste-heat recovery is constrained by the inherent trade-off between the Seebeck coefficient, electrical conductivity, and thermal conductivity in bulk semiconductor materials. High electrical conductivity (desired for low Joule heating) typically correlates with high thermal conductivity (undesired, as it shunts heat away from the junction). This study addresses the challenge of decoupling these transport properties in a cost-effective, scalable manner.
cases.reasoning_chain: The fundamental contradiction in thermoelectrics is that improving electrical conductivity (to reduce resistive losses) inherently worsens the harmful thermal factor (thermal conductivity increases, short-circuiting the temperature gradient). Classical solid-state physics suggests this is unavoidable in homogeneous materials. We propose a solution inspired by phonon-glass electron-crystal (PGEC) concept, operationalized through TRIZ. Principle #40 Composite Materials suggests creating a heterogeneous material where different constituents serve different functions. Principle #3 Local Quality mandates that each region of the material be optimized for a specific local requirement: grain interiors for high electron mobility, grain boundaries for strong phonon scattering. Principle #26 Copying indicates using a simplified, scalable nanostructured template—a 'copy' of the ideal but expensive superlattice—fabricated by bottom-up chemical synthesis. This decouples the transport parameters: electrons travel unimpeded through the crystalline grains, while phonons are scattered by the dense, atomically disordered grain boundaries, drastically reducing lattice thermal conductivity without significantly impairing electrical conductivity.
cases.solution: A nanocomposite thermoelectric material synthesized via solution-phase processing. The material consists of crystalline bismuth telluride nanograins (50-100 nm) coated with a sub-nanometer amorphous layer of a different chalcogenide. The crystalline core provides high electrical conductivity; the amorphous, acoustically mismatched boundary layer acts as a strong phonon scattering site, reducing lattice thermal conductivity to near the amorphous limit. This bulk 'copy' of a superlattice achieves a figure of merit ZT > 1.5 at 400 K, fabricated using scalable powder compaction techniques.

cases.problem: During high-speed forward flight, a fixed-wing UAV’s pitot-static airspeed sensor ices over at altitude, causing airspeed data loss and potential stall. Heating the pitot tube draws excessive electrical power, reducing endurance by 18% in cold conditions. How to prevent ice without draining the battery?
cases.reasoning_chain: Q: Can we heat the whole pitot tube all the time? A: No, that uses too much energy (worsening #20). Q: What if we only heat when ice is detected? A: That helps, but the sensor still consumes significant peak power and detection may lag. Q: Could we separate the ice prevention function from the pitot tube entirely? A: Yes, #2 Taking Out suggests extracting the problematic part—instead of preventing ice on the probe, we can measure airspeed without a physical probe exposed to icing. Q: How to do that? A: Use a laser-based optical air data system that requires no heating. Q: But lasers also consume power; can we reduce that? A: Apply #19 Periodic Action: pulse the laser at a low duty cycle (e.g., 10%) and interpolate between measurements. Q: What if residual moisture still fogs the optical window? A: Use #36 Phase Transitions: embed a small phase-change material (paraffin wax) behind the window that passively absorbs heat during the day and releases it at night to keep the window just above dew point, with no electrical power.
cases.solution: Replace pitot tube with a pulsed UV lidar air-data sensor that measures true airspeed from aerosol backscatter. The sensor operates at 10% duty cycle, consuming 0.8 W. A paraffin PCM layer behind the optical window prevents condensation/frost passively. System eliminates pitot heat entirely, saving 15 W and restoring full endurance.

cases.problem: A coordinate measuring machine (CMM) must inspect the internal geometry of a large, thin-walled aluminum casting with a required volumetric accuracy of ±5 µm. The part deflects under even light probe contact, causing measurement uncertainty that exceeds the tolerance. The probe force cannot be reduced further without losing signal integrity.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Contact probe exerts force → part deformation → measurement error. 2) Improve #28 (accuracy), worsen #10 (force). 3) Matrix (28,10) → {28, 26, 32, 3}. 4) Apply #28 Mechanics substitution: replace mechanical contact with optical probing — laser triangulation or structured light. 5) Apply #26 Copying: project a fringe pattern onto the surface and capture its deformation with a camera; the digital twin of the surface is a copy used for measurement. 6) Apply #32 Color changes: use blue-light projection to avoid ambient light interference and improve fringe contrast on shiny aluminum. 7) Result: non-contact measurement, zero probing force, ±2 µm volumetric accuracy on compliant parts.
cases.solution: Blue LED structured-light scanner with stereo cameras integrated into the CMM head. Temporal phase unwrapping and a thin anti-reflection spray coating on the casting eliminate specular reflections. The system achieves ±2 µm uncertainty without physical contact.

cases.problem: A semiconductor wafer fabrication plant requires in-situ, real-time measurement of the thickness of a transparent oxide film during chemical-mechanical planarization (CMP). The wafer is rotating at 90 rpm, pressed against a polishing pad, and flooded with slurry (abrasive particles in a chemical solution). Optical access is limited to a small window in the pad. The slurry film between the pad and wafer causes rapid, unpredictable changes in the optical path length, making standard reflectometry useless.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Context: In ophthalmology, optical coherence tomography (OCT) measures retinal thickness through the turbulent tear film. The eye moves constantly, yet micron-scale resolution is achieved. 2) Cross-domain analogy: The slurry film is like a chaotic tear film; the rotating wafer is like a saccading eye. 3) Identify contradiction: Improve accuracy (#28) of thickness measurement, but worsen adaptability (#35) because the system cannot handle slurry turbulence. 4) Matrix lookup (28,35) → {23, 35, 18, 1}. 5) Apply #23 Feedback: Use a closed-loop tracking interferometer that locks onto a spectral fringe and dynamically adjusts the reference arm to compensate for slurry path changes. 6) Apply #35 Parameter changes: Switch from a single-wavelength reflectometer to a broadband spectral interferometry (Fourier-domain) system; the entire spectrum is captured in one shot, freezing the slurry motion. 7) Apply #18 Mechanical vibration: Modulate the laser diode current at a high frequency to create a heterodyne detection scheme, shifting the measurement signal away from low-frequency slurry noise. 8) Apply #1 Segmentation: Instead of trying to measure through the thickest slurry region, segment the measurement into many short acquisitions triggered by the pad window passing over the wafer; only the clearest optical path segments are stitched together. 9) Realization: The system learns from OCT principles—parallel spectral acquisition, active feedback, and stroboscopic gating. 10) Result: A real-time CMP endpoint detector accurate to ±2 nm oxide remaining, immune to slurry turbulence.
cases.solution: A Fourier-domain optical coherence tomography (FD-OCT) system with a superluminescent diode (center 850 nm, bandwidth 50 nm) is integrated into the CMP pad. A small sapphire window in the pad provides optical access. The spectrometer acquires full A-scans at 100 kHz, synchronized with the pad rotation. Active feedback from a piezo-driven reference mirror compensates for slurry-induced path-length variations. Only data acquired when the window is directly over the wafer center is used for segmentation.

cases.problem: We present a method for measuring the residual stress profile in a shot-peened aerospace aluminum alloy (AA7075-T6) component non-destructively. The conventional technique, X-ray diffraction (XRD), requires electropolishing to remove material layer-by-layer, rendering the part unusable. A non-destructive alternative, ultrasonic velocity measurement, suffers from poor spatial resolution (>5 mm) and cannot resolve the steep stress gradient within the first 200 µm of the surface. We seek to improve the spatial resolution of the ultrasonic method to match that of XRD (<50 µm) without introducing the destructive layer removal step.
cases.reasoning_chain: The academic abstract identifies a fundamental trade-off: high spatial resolution in stress measurement is typically achieved only by destructive means (XRD + layer removal). Improving resolution (#29) worsens the harmful effect on the object (#30) because the object is consumed. The TRIZ matrix for (29,30) yields principles {18, 35, 28, 40}. Principle #18 (Mechanical vibration) suggests using surface acoustic waves (SAWs) with a frequency high enough to confine the wave energy to the top 200 µm. Principle #35 (Parameter changes) indicates switching from bulk wave velocity to frequency-dependent SAW dispersion, where the penetration depth is a function of frequency; by sweeping frequency, a depth profile is obtained without physical removal. Principle #28 (Mechanics substitution) points toward laser-generated and laser-detected ultrasound, replacing contact piezoelectric transducers that average over large areas. The resulting laser-SAW spectroscopy system achieves a depth resolution of 30 µm by analyzing the dispersion curve of broadband SAWs, acknowledging the trade-off that measurement time increases by a factor of 10 compared to bulk wave methods.
cases.solution: A laser-induced surface acoustic wave spectroscopy (LISAWS) system: a pulsed Nd:YAG laser (532 nm, 3 ns) generates broadband SAWs, and a path-stabilized Michelson interferometer detects the surface displacement. The phase velocity dispersion curve from 20–100 MHz is inverted using a genetic algorithm to solve for the depth-dependent elastic constants. The stress profile is derived from the acoustoelastic effect. Measurement spot size is 50 µm.

cases.problem: A hearing aid manufacturer receives complaints that their newest completely-in-canal (CIC) device is uncomfortable because it creates an occluded, 'plugged-up' feeling. The occlusion effect amplifies the user's own voice and chewing sounds by up to 20 dB, which is unbearable for many. The obvious solution is a large vent, but that drastically reduces low-frequency amplification and causes feedback. The device must provide high-fidelity sound without the occlusion effect.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The core trade-off: a vent reduces the occlusion effect (harmful side effect #31) but worsens sound fidelity (#26) by letting low frequencies escape and causing feedback. 2) Look up contradiction matrix for (26,31) -> principles {3, 17, 23, 40}. 3) Let's draw an analogy from architectural acoustics: concert halls use Helmholtz resonators to absorb specific frequencies without blocking the entire space. 4) Apply #17 Another Dimension: instead of a simple 1D cylindrical vent, create a 3D labyrinthine channel inside the hearing aid shell. 5) Apply #3 Local Quality: make the channel walls from a porous, frequency-selective material (#40 Composite Materials) that absorbs only the low-frequency occlusion band (100-500 Hz) while reflecting higher frequencies. 6) This passive filter still can't adapt to different ear canals. Apply #15 Dynamics: integrate a micro-electromechanical (MEMS) valve that adjusts the vent's acoustic mass based on the detected sound environment. 7) Apply #23 Feedback: an internal microphone monitors the residual occlusion signal and drives the MEMS valve in real-time to cancel it via active noise control through the vent itself. 8) The system becomes a dynamic acoustic impedance matching network. 9) The vent is no longer a simple hole but a tuned, adaptive acoustic transmission line. 10) This eliminates the occlusion effect without sacrificing bass response or introducing feedback.
cases.solution: Adaptive acoustic vent: a 3D-printed labyrinthine channel filled with a composite of porous PTFE and activated carbon acts as a passive low-frequency absorber. A MEMS variable-aperture valve, controlled by an internal DSP analyzing the occlusion signal, dynamically tunes the vent's acoustic impedance. The occlusion peak is reduced by 22 dB, while full low-frequency gain is maintained.

cases.problem: A brake rotor reaching 600°C during repeated stops exhibits a 20% friction coefficient fade, increasing stopping distance from 35 m to 42 m. The rotor mass is 8 kg. We need to eliminate fade without increasing mass or changing the pad material.
cases.reasoning_chain: Q: Why does friction fade? A: The pad resin decomposes at high temperature, creating a gas layer that lubricates the interface. Q: So the problem is gas entrapment, not just temperature? A: Exactly. Q: Could we remove the gas as it forms? A: That’s #31 Porous Materials—if the rotor itself were porous, it could vent the gases. But a porous rotor might be weaker. Q: What if we only make the friction surface porous? A: We can merge two materials using #5 Merging: a dense core for strength and a porous friction layer. Q: But the pad still sees 600°C. Can we actively cool the rotor? A: #36 Phase Transitions: embed a material in the rotor core that melts at 500°C, absorbing massive latent heat. Q: Wouldn’t that leak out? A: Encapsulate it in sealed channels within the core, like a heat pipe. The porous surface vents gas, the phase-change core absorbs heat spikes, and the dense structure maintains integrity. The rotor stays below 450°C, no fade.
cases.solution: A ventilated brake rotor with a gray cast iron core containing encapsulated sodium-filled channels (melting point 98°C, but high latent heat for heat spikes) and a laser-deposited porous stainless steel friction layer (30% porosity, pore size 50 µm). The rotor mass remains 8 kg, fade is eliminated, and stopping distance returns to 35 m.

cases.problem: A Formula 1 team needs to reduce the aerodynamic drag of the rear wing by 15% to gain straight-line speed, but the downforce must not drop by more than 3% to preserve cornering grip. The wing is currently a fixed multi-element profile.
cases.reasoning_chain: Step 1: Define the ideal final result – the wing provides high downforce in corners and low drag on straights. Step 2: The conflict is static geometry versus dynamic conditions. Step 3: In nature, birds morph their wings: a falcon tucks its wings for a dive (low drag) and spreads them for slow flight (high lift). Step 4: Apply #15 Dynamics: make the rear wing adjustable. Step 5: A simple flap (like DRS) reduces drag but also dumps downforce abruptly—dangerous in corners. Step 6: Apply #17 Another Dimension: instead of just rotating a flap, can the entire wing translate or change its angle of attack relative to the car body? Step 7: Apply #4 Asymmetry: use two separate wing elements that move independently. Step 8: Apply #19 Periodic Action: on the straight, the wings oscillate slightly in anti-phase to disrupt the low-pressure wake without fully stalling, reducing drag while maintaining some downforce. Step 9: Apply #23 Feedback: use real-time yaw rate and steering angle sensors to command the wing positions, allowing asymmetric downforce for cornering. Step 10: The system uses a central hydraulic actuator with a differential linkage. Step 11: Result: drag reduced by 16% on straights, downforce loss limited to 2.5% in corners.
cases.solution: A dual-element active rear wing with independent electro-hydraulic actuation. Each element can vary its angle of attack from 0° (minimum drag) to 35° (maximum downforce) and can also translate forward/backward by 50 mm. A control algorithm using vehicle dynamics sensors commands asymmetric angles in corners and a slight anti-phase oscillation (5 Hz, ±2°) on straights to reduce wake drag. Downforce loss is 2.5%, drag reduction is 16%.

cases.problem: A fighter jet's radar-absorbent material (RAM) coating on leading edges degrades rapidly under supersonic rain erosion at Mach 1.8. Laboratory tests show coating loss of 0.12 mm per flight hour, requiring reapplication every 50 flight hours. Increasing coating thickness to 3 mm adds 45 kg and reduces stealth by increasing surface roughness. Can we maintain low radar cross-section (RCS) while surviving rain erosion?
cases.reasoning_chain: So the problem is: we want the coating to last longer (improve durability #15), but making it thicker ruins the aerodynamic shape and stealth properties (worsen #12). Counterfactual: What if the coating wasn't uniform? What if we only put the thick, durable material exactly where the rain impacts, and keep the rest thin for stealth? That's Local Quality (#3). But rain erosion is worst on leading edges, which are curved. Could we make the coating itself curved in a way that deflects water? Spheroidality (#14) suggests using curved, hydrophobic microstructures. What if the protective layer is sacrificial and continuously renewed? Discarding and Recovering (#34) — like an ablative heat shield, but for rain. Could we use a composite (#40) with a hard ceramic outer skin that erodes slowly while the inner layer absorbs radar?
cases.solution: Graded RAM leading-edge system: a thin, dense ceramic outer layer (silicon oxycarbide) with microscale hemispherical protrusions (Cassie-Baxter wetting state) sheds water droplets before impact; beneath it, a conventional magnetic RAM layer. The ceramic is applied only to leading edges (local quality) and is designed as a replaceable appliqué strip, discarded and replaced during routine maintenance without affecting the underlying RAM.

cases.problem: A long-endurance solar-powered UAV (unmanned aerial vehicle) requires large wing area for lift and solar collection, but the resulting high aspect-ratio wing (35 m span) is extremely flexible and prone to aeroelastic flutter at speeds above 25 m/s, limiting dash capability to escape adverse weather. Stiffening the wing adds mass, reducing payload to zero. The flutter boundary must be pushed beyond 40 m/s without weight penalty.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The UAV must fly faster to escape storms (improve #9 Speed). 2) Higher speed induces flutter, degrading structural stability (worsen #13). 3) Matrix (9,13) → {6, 7, 18, 23, 28, 36}. 4) Analogy: Consider how a spider's web resists wind — not by stiffness but by tension and active adjustment. 5) Apply #6 Universality: make the wing structure serve multiple functions — it should carry load, harvest energy, and sense flutter simultaneously. 6) Apply #7 Nested Doll: embed a network of thin, pressurized pneumatic channels inside the composite wing skin. 7) Apply #18 Mechanical Vibration: use out-of-phase piezoelectric actuators to actively cancel flutter modes. 8) Apply #23 Feedback: fiber Bragg grating sensors embedded in the spar detect onset of flutter in real time. 9) Apply #28 Mechanics Substitution: replace passive stiffness with active aerodynamic control via micro-tabs on the trailing edge, driven by sensor signals. 10) Apply #36 Phase Transitions: the pneumatic channels contain a low-boiling-point fluid; when flutter is detected, the fluid is flash-evaporated, increasing internal pressure and stiffening the wing instantly (like an inflatable structure). 11) This multi-physics system allows the wing to be flexible in normal flight (lightweight) and rigid only when needed, pushing flutter speed to 45 m/s with no mass increase.
cases.solution: Smart adaptive wing: a lightweight CFRP wing with embedded fiber optic strain sensors, piezoelectric patch actuators, and a network of microchannels containing a hydrofluoroether fluid. Onboard controller detects flutter precursors; activates piezoelectric damping and triggers rapid vaporization in channels, increasing wing bending stiffness by 300% within 0.3 seconds. Flutter boundary shifts from 25 m/s to 45 m/s without adding structural mass.

cases.problem: Abstract: Hypersonic glide vehicles experience extreme aerodynamic heating (surface temperatures >2000°C) during sustained flight at Mach 10. Active cooling systems (e.g., regenerative fuel cooling) consume substantial payload mass fraction and introduce failure modes. Passive thermal protection systems (TPS) are heavy and ablative, altering vehicle shape and thus aerodynamic performance. A fundamental trade-off exists between maintaining vehicle shape integrity (low ablation) and minimizing TPS mass. We seek a material system that provides thermal protection without shape change or excessive mass.
cases.reasoning_chain: The core contradiction: to withstand high temperature (improve #17), we typically add heavy, ablative material which worsens weight (#2). The matrix for (17,2) yields principles {9, 11, 22, 31, 37}. We acknowledge that any passive solid TPS will always trade mass for performance. Instead, we reframe the problem: can we use the heat itself to create a protective effect? Preliminary Anti-Action (#9) suggests pre-stressing the material to counteract thermal expansion. Beforehand Cushioning (#11) implies preparing a sacrificial layer that activates only when needed. Blessing in Disguise (#22) asks: can the harmful heat be turned into a benefit? Porous Materials (#31) offers a lightweight substrate. Thermal Expansion (#37) could be harnessed. Our solution: a porous ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) matrix infiltrated with a low-melting-point metal. At operational temperature, the metal melts and exudes through the pores via capillary action, forming a thin, continuously replenished liquid film on the surface. This film evaporatively cools the vehicle (transpiration cooling) and the vapor boundary layer reduces convective heating. The film is self-healing and mass loss is minimal. The porous UHTC provides shape stability. The system uses the heat itself to pump the coolant — no active pumps required. Thus, we achieve high-temperature capability with significantly lower system mass than ablative TPS, accepting a small but tolerable mass loss rate.
cases.solution: Self-regulating transpiration-cooled UHTC composite: a porous zirconium diboride (ZrB2) matrix is infiltrated with gallium. Above 30°C (gallium melting point), the liquid metal wicks to the surface and evaporates, forming a protective vapor film. The evaporation rate self-adjusts with heat flux, providing cooling exactly where needed. Surface remains dimensionally stable. System mass is 60% less than equivalent ablative TPS for a 30-minute Mach 10 trajectory.

cases.problem: In powder-bed fusion (PBF) metal 3D printing, residual stresses from steep thermal gradients cause part distortion and cracking, requiring stress-relief heat treatment that increases lead time by 8-12 hours.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) High thermal gradients induce residual stress, causing warping. 2) Improve #11 (Stress), worsen #21 (Speed). 3) Matrix (11,21) → {10, 15, 36, 35}. 4) Apply #10 Preliminary Action: pre-heat the build plate and powder bed to a uniform elevated temperature before scanning begins, reducing the delta-T. 5) Apply #15 Dynamics: dynamically adjust laser power and scan speed based on local geometry to maintain a consistent cooling rate. 6) Apply #36 Phase Transitions: use a powder alloy that undergoes a solid-state phase transformation during cooling, counteracting tensile stresses with compressive ones. 7) The combined effect minimizes distortion in-situ, eliminating the need for a separate post-process stress relief cycle.
cases.solution: In-situ stress management for PBF: build plate preheated to 200°C, closed-loop laser parameter modulation based on thermal camera feedback, and use of a transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP) steel powder. Parts are built net-shape without post-process stress relief, reducing total cycle time by 35%.

cases.problem: Electric vehicle traction motors generate peak torque of 480 Nm but suffer a 12% efficiency drop at sustained high RPM due to eddy-current heating in the rotor magnets, forcing a 40 kg liquid cooling jacket that adds assembly steps.
cases.reasoning_chain:
Q: We need more power density, but cooling adds weight. What if we don't cool it at all?
A: Then the magnets demagnetize above 150°C and we lose torque permanently.
Q: So we must remove heat. What if we take the heat out without liquid?
A: Principle #2 Taking Out suggests removing the troublesome part—the magnets—from the hot rotor entirely. What if we put them in the stator?
Q: That's a switched reluctance motor, but it has torque ripple. Could we keep permanent magnets but still separate them from the heat source?
A: Yes, principle #14 Spheroidality - Curvature: instead of flat magnets glued to a cylindrical rotor, use spherical magnet powder suspended in a high-temperature polymer matrix, injection-molded into a Halbach array. The spherical shape minimizes eddy-current loops.
Q: But it still gets hot. How to cool it without a heavy jacket?
A: Principle #36 Phase Transitions: embed micro-encapsulated phase-change material (PCM) directly into the rotor core. The PCM melts at 120°C, absorbing peak heat during high-RPM bursts, and re-solidifies during low-load city driving. The latent heat of fusion handles the transient spikes, so we only need a small air-cooling channel. The heavy liquid jacket is eliminated, rotor weight drops by 35%, and continuous power rating increases 15%.
cases.solution: A permanent-magnet rotor using spherical NdFeB powder in a polymer Halbach array to suppress eddy currents, with integrated micro-encapsulated PCM (paraffin wax) that undergoes solid-liquid phase transition at 120°C to absorb peak thermal loads. A lightweight air-cooling duct replaces the liquid cooling jacket.

cases.problem: Urban air mobility (UAM) eVTOL aircraft require noise levels below 55 dB(A) at 100 m altitude to gain community acceptance, but the high tip speed (210 m/s) of the distributed electric rotors generates broadband noise peaking at 72 dB(A) in the 1–4 kHz range, which is psychoacoustically annoying.
cases.reasoning_chain: We present a study addressing the noise-speed trade-off in multirotor eVTOL configurations. Reducing rotor tip speed (improving parameter #24, Noise) directly compromises cruise speed and thus mission productivity (worsening parameter #9, Speed). The classical contradiction matrix yields inventive principles #4 (Asymmetry), #17 (Another Dimension), and #26 (Copying). Principle #17 is applied by stacking two counter-rotating rotors in a coaxial pair, but with a non-uniform axial spacing that varies azimuthally—the upper rotor is tilted 2° relative to the lower, creating a helical interaction pattern that breaks up coherent tonal noise. Principle #4 Asymmetry is implemented on each blade: the trailing edge is serrated with a non-periodic, fractal-like profile derived from owl-wing geometry, which scatters turbulent boundary layer eddies into higher frequencies that are rapidly attenuated by atmospheric absorption. Principle #26 Copying is used not for physical replication but for acoustic cancellation: a ring of MEMS microphones around the duct senses the residual blade-passing frequency and drives a lightweight piezoelectric actuator on the duct inner wall to generate an anti-phase acoustic copy, reducing the fundamental tone by 12 dB. We acknowledge the trade-off: the serrations increase profile drag by 2.3%, reducing range by 4 km for a given battery. However, the combined solution achieves a community noise level of 52 dB(A) at 100 m while maintaining a 260 km/h cruise speed, meeting regulatory thresholds without the weight penalty of full ducted fans.
cases.solution: Coaxial rotor pair with 2° relative tilt and fractal serrated trailing edges, integrated with a duct-mounted active noise cancellation system using MEMS microphones and piezoelectric drivers. The asymmetric rotor phasing and biomimetic edge treatment reduce tonal and broadband noise by 20 dB(A), while the slight drag penalty is offset by the elimination of heavy acoustic insulation.

cases.problem: A reaction wheel assembly (RWA) for an Earth-observation satellite must deliver a torque of 0.25 N·m to slew the spacecraft rapidly for target acquisition, but the peak power draw must not exceed 90 W from the 28 V unregulated bus.

contradiction_pair.worsening_parameter: Use of energy by moving object (#19)

cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Higher torque requires higher motor current; motor current ∝ torque constant Kt; power ∝ I²R + back-EMF losses. 2) Improve #10 (torque), worsen #19 (power consumed). 3) Matrix (10,19) → {19, 14, 15, 8}. 4) Apply #19 Periodic Action: torque is not needed continuously—slew is a short transient. Design the motor to deliver peak torque in a 2 s burst with a 30 s cool-down duty cycle, sizing the thermal mass to absorb the pulse. 5) Apply #14 Spheroidality - Curvature: use a curved Halbach magnet array to increase flux density in the air gap without adding mass, raising Kt per ampere. 6) Apply #15 Dynamics: implement a variable-bus-voltage driver that boosts the 28 V rail to 48 V only during the torque pulse, reducing I²R losses for the same power. 7) Result: 0.25 N·m peak torque, 85 W peak power, steady-state dissipation <15 W.
cases.solution: A pulse-rated reaction wheel with a Halbach rotor and a dynamically boosted bus driver; the wheel accelerates to slew speed in 2 s, then coasts with only bearing drag compensation, keeping average power under 90 W.

cases.problem: A cryogenic propellant tank for a lunar lander must hold liquid oxygen at 90 K for a 14-day surface stay, but the tank wall must be as light as possible because every kilogram of dry mass costs 5 kg of propellant in the descent stage. Multi-layer insulation (MLI) works in vacuum but is heavy and bulky; bare metal tanks suffer unacceptable boil-off.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The core contradiction: improve #2 (reduce tank mass), worsen #17 (temperature rise from heat leak). 2) Matrix (2,17) → {31, 36, 35, 9, 22, 17, 21, 4, 24, 34}. 3) Cross-domain analogy: in cryogenic electron microscopy, samples are kept cold by a continuous flow of liquid nitrogen through a porous copper block — the latent heat of vaporization absorbs the heat leak. 4) Apply #31 Porous Materials: line the tank interior with an open-cell metal foam that wicks liquid oxygen. 5) Apply #36 Phase Transitions: as heat leaks through the thin tank wall, it vaporizes the LOX in the foam; the vapor is vented, carrying away the heat. The tank wall can be ultra-thin (0.5 mm aluminum) because it doesn't need to support pressure — it's vented. 6) Apply #35 Parameter Changes: operate the tank at ambient pressure (vented), so the wall only handles hydrostatic head, not pressure. 7) Apply #9 Preliminary Anti-Action: pre-chill the foam with a small nitrogen purge before filling to reduce initial boil-off. 8) Apply #22 Blessing in Disguise: the vented gaseous oxygen is used for attitude control thrusters, turning a loss into a resource. 9) Apply #17 Another Dimension: shape the foam as concentric rings with radial channels to control slosh. 10) Apply #21 Skipping: the vent valve operates in a pulsed mode, opening only when pressure exceeds a threshold, reducing total vented mass. 11) Apply #4 Asymmetry: the foam is thicker on the sun-facing side to handle higher heat flux. 12) Apply #24 Intermediary: a thin aluminized Mylar layer between foam and tank wall acts as a radiation barrier. 13) Apply #34 Discarding and Recovering: the foam is a sacrificial layer that slowly erodes as LOX boils; it's designed to be partially consumed. 14) Result: tank dry mass <15 kg for 500 kg LOX capacity, boil-off <2% per day, vent gas provides 50 N attitude control thrust.
cases.solution: A vented, foam-lined LOX tank: a thin aluminum shell (0.5 mm) lined with graded open-cell aluminum foam that wicks liquid oxygen. Heat leak vaporizes LOX in the foam; the vapor is vented through a pulsed valve and fed to cold-gas attitude thrusters. The foam is thicker on the sunward side and pre-chilled before filling. Boil-off is converted into useful impulse.

cases.problem: Spacecraft reaction control thrusters (RCS) using hydrazine monopropellant must deliver impulse bits as small as 0.1 N·s for precision pointing, but the solenoid valves exhibit a minimum on-time of 10 ms due to mechanical inertia, yielding a minimum impulse of 0.5 N·s at nominal thrust. Reducing thrust by lowering chamber pressure compromises propellant decomposition efficiency and specific impulse (Isp).
cases.reasoning_chain: The RCS thruster must produce a very small impulse for fine pointing, but the valve cannot open and close fast enough to deliver such a small impulse at full thrust. The contradiction is between improving the precision of the applied force (impulse bit) and worsening the speed of the actuation system. In TRIZ terms, we improve #10 (Force) — specifically, the ability to generate a very small, precise force impulse — but this worsens #9 (Speed) because the mechanical valve is too slow. Consulting the contradiction matrix for (10,9) yields principles {19, 16, 28, 31}. Principle #19 Periodic Action suggests breaking the single long pulse into a high-frequency train of very short pulses. However, the valve itself cannot switch that fast. Principle #16 Partial or Excessive Actions suggests using an 'excessive' thrust level (full thrust for high Isp) but applying it for a fraction of the required impulse, then diverting or canceling the excess. Principle #28 Mechanics Substitution points toward replacing the mechanical valve with a non-mechanical flow control. The solution is a two-stage approach: a fast-acting piezoelectric micro-valve (sub-millisecond response) is placed in series with the main solenoid valve. The main valve opens for a guaranteed minimum 10 ms, but the piezo-valve chops this flow into a precise number of 0.5 ms pulses, each delivering 0.025 N·s. The total impulse is the sum of these micro-pulses. The piezo-valve operates at 2 kHz, well within its capability. This avoids the Isp penalty of low chamber pressure because the thruster always operates at its design point during each pulse. The trade-off is increased complexity and a small pressure drop across the piezo-valve, but the impulse resolution improves by a factor of 20.
cases.solution: A dual-valve RCS thruster: a conventional solenoid isolation valve provides bulk propellant flow, while an in-line piezoelectric micro-valve chops the flow into 0.5 ms pulses at 2 kHz, achieving 0.025 N·s impulse resolution without degrading Isp.

cases.problem: At the mill, we're rolling aluminum sheet down to 0.2 mm final gauge. The operators have to spray a kerosene-based rolling oil to cool the work rolls and lubricate the bite, but the mist is terrible — it hangs in the air, coats everything, and we're exceeding VOC limits. If we cut back the oil, the rolls overheat and the sheet comes out with chatter marks and bad surface finish.
cases.reasoning_chain: We need cooling and lubrication but hate the airborne mist. That's a contradiction: we want to reduce the harmful factor (oil mist pollution) but doing so makes the rolling process unreliable due to overheating and friction. So we're improving #30 Harmful factors, but worsening #27 Reliability. The matrix says to look at #2 Taking out, #3 Local quality, #30 Flexible shells, #33 Homogeneity. What if we take out the cooling function from the oil entirely? Apply #2 Taking out: separate cooling and lubrication. Use a different, non-misting coolant for the rolls and a minimal amount of lubricant applied only where needed. For the coolant, #30 Flexible shells and thin films suggests a thin, conformal cooling interface. What if we use a rotating wiper pad that applies a thin water film to the roll surface, which evaporates cleanly? Then for lubrication, #33 Homogeneity: use a solid lubricant embedded in the aluminum itself? Too expensive. Instead, apply a microscopic oil film via an electrostatic deposition system that charges oil droplets so they adhere precisely to the strip edge before the bite, with zero overspray. The cooling is done by water-moistened fabric rolls contacting the work rolls. No mist, no VOCs, reliable rolling.
cases.solution: Hybrid cooling-lubrication system: work rolls are cooled by contact with continuously wetted fabric rolls using recirculated water; lubrication is applied as a precisely metered, electrostatically deposited oil film on the strip edges only. Mist is eliminated, VOC compliant, and surface quality is maintained at full speed.

cases.problem: My new foldable tablet has a gorgeous OLED screen, but after six months the crease in the middle looks permanently brighter and colors are washed out. Turns out the micro-lenses in the pixel array compress and deform under repeated folding pressure, changing the light extraction angle. The manufacturer says they can't make the lenses harder without making them brittle and prone to cracking. How can we stop the optical degradation without sacrificing mechanical flexibility?
cases.reasoning_chain: We start with a user complaint about crease optical degradation. The core issue is that polymer micro-lenses permanently deform under cyclic compressive folding stress. If we make the lenses harder (higher cross-link density), they resist deformation but become brittle and crack. So we want to maintain the lens shape (improve #12) without increasing internal stress (#11 worsens). Consulting the contradiction matrix for (12,11) yields principles 14, 31, 35, 13. Principle #14 Spheroidality suggests using a curved surface to distribute load evenly. Instead of hemispherical lenses, we design lenses with a toroidal or aspherical profile that has a larger radius of curvature at the apex, reducing peak contact pressure during folding. Principle #31 Porous materials leads us to introduce nano-voids in the lens polymer; these voids act as compliant cushions that collapse elastically under compression and recover when unfolded, absorbing strain without permanent deformation. Principle #35 Parameter Changes: we shift the glass transition temperature (Tg) of the lens polymer to be just above operating temperature, so the material is in a leathery viscoelastic state during folding, allowing molecular relaxation that prevents stress buildup. Together, the lenses maintain their optical shape over hundreds of thousands of folds.
