cases.solution: OLED pixel micro-lenses fabricated from a porous, low-Tg (≈60°C) fluoropolymer with an aspherical profile (conic constant -0.5). Nano-voids (20-50 nm) introduced via solvent-induced phase separation, creating 15% porosity. During folding at 25-40°C, polymer chains relax within seconds; voids compress elastically up to 30% strain. Aspherical shape reduces contact area by 40% compared to hemispherical lenses. Accelerated fold testing (200k cycles) shows <2% luminance shift at the crease, versus 18% for standard hemispherical hard lenses.

cases.problem: A foldable e-reader with a color e-paper display suffers from image ghosting after partial page updates because the microcapsule-based electrophoretic ink particles get stuck in agglomerates near the fold crease, where mechanical pressure during folding forces capsules against each other. Reducing particle size improves dispersion but increases the driving voltage from 15V to 30V, draining the battery faster.

contradiction_pair.worsening_parameter: Power consumption / Energy spent by moving object (#19)

cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The problem: electrophoretic ink particles agglomerate under fold pressure, causing ghosting. 2) We want to reduce ghosting (improve #33 ease of operation, since ghosting requires manual refresh actions) without increasing power (worsening #19). 3) Matrix lookup (33,19) → {1, 13, 28, 35, 2}. 4) Let's draw an analogy to anti-clumping in food powders (like powdered sugar) where they use anti-caking agents. 5) Apply #1 Segmentation: instead of solid pigment particles, use hollow or porous particles that are less dense and less prone to compaction. 6) Apply #13 The Other Way Round: instead of pushing particles through a liquid, vibrate the capsule walls to fluidize the particles. 7) Apply #28 Mechanics Substitution: use acoustic or electrostatic fields to keep particles separated, replacing mechanical dispersion. 8) Apply #35 Parameter Changes: change the viscosity of the suspending fluid to be shear-thinning, so it's thick at rest but thin under shear during switching. 9) Apply #2 Taking Out: remove the problematic fold-area capsules entirely and replace them with a different display technology that doesn't use particles, like electrowetting, only in that narrow strip. 10) Synthesize: segment the pigment into porous micro-grains coated with a charged surfactant; use a shear-thinning fluid; embed piezoelectric transducers in the backplane to generate ultrasonic standing waves only during page updates to fluidize the bed; in the fold zone, use a segmented electrowetting line that matches the e-paper background color. 11) Result: ghosting eliminated, driving voltage remains at 15V, no battery life penalty.
cases.solution: Segmented electrophoretic display: pigment particles are mesoporous silica shells (500 nm) filled with titanium dioxide cores, coated with a long-chain steric stabilizer. Suspending fluid is a shear-thinning silicone oil (viscosity 100 mPa·s at rest, 10 mPa·s under 1 s⁻¹ shear). Piezoelectric thin-film transducers (PZT, 1 mm pitch) integrated into the backplane emit 2 MHz ultrasound during refresh cycles to fluidize the particle bed via acoustic streaming. In the 2 mm fold corridor, microcapsules are replaced by an electrowetting pixel strip filled with dyed oil matching the white state, addressed synchronously. Driving voltage maintained at 15V; ghosting <0.5 ΔE* after 100k folds; power increase <3%.

cases.problem: A high-speed pill-coating drum must apply a uniform sugar layer to 100,000 tablets per hour. Current spray nozzles clog with crystallized sugar, requiring frequent line stoppages for cleaning, reducing overall equipment effectiveness to 65%.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Increase spray rate to boost throughput, but sugar crystallizes on nozzle tips, causing blockage and unreliable coating. 2) The contradiction: Productivity (#39) vs. Reliability (#27). 3) Apply #35 Parameter Changes: alter the physical state of the coating medium—use a super-saturated sugar solution maintained just above crystallization temperature within the nozzle. 4) Apply #10 Preliminary Action: pre-heat the nozzles and coat their inner surfaces with a thin film of food-grade oil before spraying begins to delay nucleation. 5) Apply #24 Intermediary: introduce a pulsed, high-pressure air stream through the nozzle between spray cycles to eject any forming crystals without stopping production. 6) The combined solution prevents clogging during continuous operation, restoring reliability at high throughput.
cases.solution: Pre-heat spray nozzles and coat internals with a thin oil film before startup. Use a heated, super-saturated sugar solution and pulse compressed air through nozzles between spray cycles to dislodge nascent crystals, enabling continuous high-speed coating without clogging.

cases.problem: Bug Report ID: HVAC-2024-07. In the 12-story mixed-use building, the central VAV system maintains setpoints within ±0.5°C, but 23% of occupants on the south-facing perimeter still report thermal discomfort (drafts) during winter. Increasing supply air temperature to compensate overheats the core zones by 3°C above setpoint.
cases.reasoning_chain: Q: We need a system that adapts to perimeter heat loss (improve #35 Adaptability) but adding individual zone dampers and controllers would explode the complexity (worsen #36 Device complexity). How can we get zonal control without a complex mechanical system? A: What if the air itself could adapt? Q: Like smart air? A: Not smart, but responsive. Principle #1 Segmentation says divide the airflow, but not mechanically. Principle #28 Mechanics Substitution suggests replacing mechanics with a field. Principle #15 Dynamics says make it adjustable. Q: So, a field that steers air? A: Exactly. Use a dielectric barrier discharge plasma actuator embedded in the diffuser. By applying a high-voltage AC signal, it ionizes air and creates an electrohydrodynamic body force that can vector the supply jet. No moving parts. The diffuser can dynamically switch between a wide throw for core cooling and a focused, high-velocity jet that hugs the perimeter ceiling to wash the cold window surface, countering drafts without overheating the core.
cases.solution: Install plasma-actuated slot diffusers along the perimeter. A 10 kVpp AC signal at 5 kHz ionizes air, creating an EHD force that steers the supply jet. In winter mode, the jet is vectored to adhere to the cold facade (Coanda effect), creating a warm air curtain. Core zone temperature deviation eliminated; thermal comfort complaints drop to <5%.

cases.problem: Abstract: Museum conservation standards require strict control of illuminance (≤50 lux for watercolours) and UV to prevent photochemical degradation. However, the architectural desire for a visually open, daylit gallery creates a fundamental conflict. Conventional solutions (UV-filtering laminated glass, fixed louvers) either fail to meet the dynamic lux limits during peak sun or sever the visual connection to the exterior, compromising the curatorial narrative of contextual art.
cases.reasoning_chain: The primary goal is to prevent artifact degradation by minimizing substance loss (improve #23 Loss of substance) from photochemical reactions, but the fundamental requirement for viewing is illumination, which inherently carries the damaging energy (worsen #18 Illumination intensity). This is a classic trade-off: we cannot eliminate light without eliminating vision. The TRIZ matrix for (23,18) yields principles 13, 37, and 24. Instead of trying to filter the light at the source (the window), principle #13 (The Other Way Round) suggests inverting the problem: make the artwork itself the dynamic filter. We acknowledge the trade-off by decoupling the viewing light from the damaging light in the time domain. Principle #24 (Intermediary) introduces a sacrificial optical shuttle. A transparent, thermochromic display panel (using principle #37 Thermal Expansion of microcapsules) is placed in front of the artwork. When a visitor is absent, the panel is opaque, blocking all light. When a presence sensor detects a viewer, a localized, projector-based illumination system (the intermediary) projects a high-fidelity image of the artwork onto the now-transparent panel for a brief 15-second interval. The actual artwork remains in near-darkness, receiving only the brief, controlled, and reflected light, thus drastically reducing cumulative lux-hours while preserving the visual experience.
cases.solution: Implement a viewer-activated dynamic display system: a thermochromic smart-glass panel in front of the artwork remains opaque (0.1% transmittance) when no viewer is present. A PIR sensor triggers a 15-second cycle where the panel becomes transparent and a calibrated LED projector (CRI 98, 45 lux at surface) illuminates the artwork. Cumulative daily lux-hours for the artifact are reduced by 98%, while the visitor perceives a naturally lit, contextual piece.

cases.problem: Specification: A high-volume PCB assembly line uses a wave soldering machine with a 300 kg solder pot. The pot must be maintained at 250±2°C. After a weekend shutdown, the solid ingot requires 4 hours to fully melt and reach thermal equilibrium, delaying Monday morning production start by 3 hours. Pre-heating the pot earlier is not feasible due to unattended-operation fire safety regulations. Design a system that reduces the startup time to under 30 minutes without increasing the peak power draw beyond the existing 15 kW limit, and without compromising thermal uniformity.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The core issue is the long time needed to melt a 300 kg block of solid solder from room temperature. 2) We want to improve Speed (#9) of reaching operational state, but this normally worsens Power (#21) because faster melting requires more kW. 3) Matrix lookup for (9,21) yields principles {10, 35, 36, 2}. 4) Step 1: Apply #10 Preliminary Action — instead of a single massive ingot, pre-cast the solder into small, standardized 2 kg pellets during the previous shutdown using a quick-release mold. These pellets have a much higher surface-area-to-volume ratio. 5) Step 2: Apply #36 Phase Transitions — exploit the latent heat of fusion. Store the pellets in an insulated hopper directly above the pot. Use a small, dedicated 2 kW induction coil to melt a 'seed pool' of just 2 kg of pellets in 90 seconds. 6) Step 3: Apply #35 Parameter Changes — change the physical state of the bulk material. Once the seed pool is molten, a heated auger feeds the remaining solid pellets into the pool at a controlled rate. The pellets melt almost instantly upon contact with the turbulent liquid, leveraging the high thermal conductivity of the liquid phase. 7) The 15 kW main heaters only need to maintain the pot temperature and supply the latent heat for the incoming pellets, never exceeding the power limit. 8) Result: Full 300 kg molten pot ready in <25 minutes using the existing 15 kW infrastructure.
cases.solution: A segmented solder delivery system: solder is cast into 2 kg pellets during cooldown. A 2 kW induction seed-pool melter creates an initial liquid bath in 90 seconds. A temperature-controlled auger then feeds pellets into the bath, where they melt instantly. The main 15 kW heaters maintain thermal equilibrium. Startup time is reduced from 240 minutes to under 25 minutes, with zero increase in peak power demand.

cases.problem: Bug Report ID: SMT-PnP-8842. Machine: High-speed pick-and-place (P&P) line for 0201 metric components (0.2 x 0.1 mm). Nozzle type: Standard ceramic 0.15 mm ID. Issue: Component pickup reliability is 99.95% (target >99.99%). Root cause traced to nozzle clogging by airborne solder paste flux fumes. The flux volatilizes during reflow upstream, and sticky residue accumulates inside the nozzle tip, reducing vacuum over ~5000 placements. Current fix: Stop production every 4 hours for a 15-minute manual nozzle cleaning cycle (alcohol wipe + ultrasonic bath). This reduces line utilization by 6.25%. Increasing vacuum pressure to overcome partial clogs causes the tiny 0201 parts to stick to the nozzle and fail to place (blow-off error). We need to prevent flux residue buildup inside the nozzle without stopping the machine and without using a higher vacuum pressure that causes placement errors.
cases.reasoning_chain: Let's break this down. The flux fumes are an external contaminant that we want to stop from adhering to the internal nozzle wall. We can't easily filter the whole machine's air, so we must protect the nozzle locally. 1) The contaminant is airborne and sticky. 2) We want to prevent material loss (vacuum pressure) due to clogging, but the current fix causes time loss for cleaning. 3) The contradiction matrix for 'Loss of substance' vs. 'Time loss' gives principles: {15, 10, 34, 28}. 4) Let's apply #10 Preliminary Action. Before the nozzle ever contacts the component, we can pre-treat its internal surface. A micro-dispenser applies a thin coating of a sacrificial, non-stick fluoropolymer (like a dry PTFE spray) to the inner bore of each nozzle during the initial setup. This makes the surface oleophobic and prevents flux residue from sticking. 5) Next, apply #24 Intermediary. We can introduce a consumable barrier. Instead of coating the nozzle, we could use a 'sacrificial tip'—a short, disposable peek tube inserted into the nozzle tip that gets replaced automatically. But that adds bulk. Better: the vacuum air itself can carry the intermediary. A small atomizer injects a minute amount of an inert, food-grade, anti-tack liquid (like a light silicone oil) into the vacuum line as an aerosol. This forms a transient, molecular-thin liquid film on the nozzle wall that flux fumes cannot bond to. 6) Finally, apply #34 Discarding and Recovering. The anti-tack film, carrying any flux that did manage to condense, is continuously evaporated and exhausted by the vacuum pump. The nozzle effectively cleans itself on every vacuum cycle. 7) The combined solution: a PTFE-like pre-coating for the nozzle bore, continuously replenished by a trace aerosol of a volatile anti-tack agent in the vacuum line, which is then discarded. The nozzle never clogs, eliminating the 6.25% downtime and the need for higher, error-prone vacuum.
cases.solution: A self-maintaining nozzle system: Nozzle interiors are pre-coated with a permanent oleophobic nanocoating. A micro-atomizer injects a trace amount of volatile, inert anti-tack liquid (e.g., medical-grade cyclic silicone) into the vacuum line. This forms a transient, non-stick molecular film on the nozzle wall that prevents flux adhesion and is continuously evacuated. The nozzle remains clog-free for >1 million placements, eliminating the 15-minute cleaning cycles and allowing a lower, safe vacuum pressure.

cases.problem: Customer complaint: 'Your new laptop's magnesium alloy chassis is great—light and strong—but it's freezing cold when I first open it in the morning, and the sharp edges around the USB-C ports are uncomfortable on my wrists. My old plastic laptop never felt like this. Can you make a metal laptop that feels warm and smooth like plastic, but is still as tough and thin?'
cases.reasoning_chain: This is a classic user-experience contradiction. The desirable property is a comfortable, organic shape (improving 'Shape') without the undesirable property of high thermal conductivity making it feel cold (worsening 'Temperature'). Let's solve this with a cross-domain analogy: a high-performance ski. A ski needs a sharp, hard metal edge for carving ice, but a soft, damp core for a smooth ride. It's a multi-layer composite. 1) We start with the magnesium alloy structural frame for strength and thinness. 2) Apply #40 Composite Materials. We don't make the whole bottom case from one material. We create a hybrid: a 0.4 mm stamped magnesium frame is insert-molded with a 1.0 mm layer of thermally-insulating, glass-fiber-reinforced thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU). The metal provides the EMC shielding and rigidity; the plastic provides the warm touch and impact damping. 3) Apply #30 Flexible Shells and Thin Films. The sharp port edges are a local shape problem. On the magnesium frame, the USB-C cutouts are punched. We apply a thin, flexible TPU film over the entire inner surface, which wraps around the edges of the cutouts, creating a soft, rolled lip. This is like the edge seal on a car door. 4) Apply #3 Local Quality. The wrist rest area needs to be the warmest. Here, the TPU overmold is formulated with hollow glass microspheres to increase its insulative R-value by 300% compared to the rest of the base, without changing the overall thickness. The structure is locally optimized for thermal comfort. 5) Apply #14 Spheroidality - Curvature. The sharp edges are a stress concentrator and a comfort issue. Instead of a sharp 90-degree punch, the magnesium frame's edges are hydroformed to a 2 mm radius curve before overmolding. The plastic then flows over this curve, creating a perfectly smooth, continuous surface that transitions from the bottom to the side wall. 6) The final result is a unibody-feel chassis that is structurally magnesium, thermally plastic, and ergonomically curved, with no sharp edges. The user perceives the warmth of plastic and the premium rigidity of metal.
cases.solution: A hybrid metal-plastic chassis. A thin, curved magnesium alloy structural frame (0.4 mm, 2 mm edge radii) provides EMC shielding and rigidity. This frame is insert-molded with a thermally-insulating TPU layer (1.0 mm), which wraps around port cutouts to create soft lips. The palm-rest zone uses a TPU variant with hollow glass microspheres for 3x higher thermal resistance. The result is a thin, strong chassis that feels warm and smooth to the touch, with no sharp edges.

fail_reasons: The contradiction pair uses 'Shape (#12)' and 'Temperature (#17)', but the canonical names for these parameters are not correct. The correct parameters should be 'Shape' and 'Temperature' without the numbers.

cases.problem: So we've got this electric bus that runs a fixed route in the city, and the fleet manager is complaining that the battery pack dies way too fast in winter. The cabin heater sucks 40% of the battery just keeping passengers warm, but if we add more batteries, the bus gets too heavy and we lose passenger capacity. We're stuck between a freezing bus and a stranded bus.
cases.reasoning_chain: Okay, let's walk through this. The core issue is that the cabin heater is a massive electrical load. We want to improve cabin temperature (passenger comfort) without adding more battery weight. The contradiction matrix for improving Temperature (#17) vs. worsening Weight of moving object (#1) gives us principles #36 Phase Transitions, #22 Blessing in Disguise, and #35 Parameter Changes. What if we don't fight the cold with electricity? Using #36, we can install a phase-change material (PCM) under the floor that absorbs heat from the motors and power electronics during operation, storing it as latent heat. Then, during stops or overnight charging, it releases that heat passively into the cabin. But wait, that's not enough for peak cold. For #22 Blessing in Disguise, we turn the battery's own waste heat—normally a problem—into a solution: route the battery thermal management system's coolant through a heat exchanger to pre-warm cabin air. The 'harm' (battery heat) becomes a resource. Finally, #35 Parameter Changes: we switch from resistive heating to a heat pump, which has a COP of 3-4, drastically reducing the electrical load for the same thermal output. Combined, we cut heater energy by 70% without adding a single kilogram of battery.
cases.solution: A tri-modal thermal system: (1) Paraffin-based PCM panels under the floor store waste heat from traction motors and inverters during operation, releasing it during dwell times. (2) A coolant-to-air heat exchanger recovers battery pack waste heat for cabin preheating. (3) A reversible heat pump replaces the resistive PTC heater, achieving a COP of 3.5 at -10°C. Total cabin heating energy reduced from 4.2 kWh to 1.1 kWh per route cycle, eliminating the need for a larger battery pack.

cases.problem: A raise-boring reamer head jams and stalls at 320 m depth in granodiorite. Downhole camera shows a 1.2 m diameter boulder wedged between the reamer body and the pilot hole wall. Torque spikes exceed 450 kN·m, risking drill string failure. Standard fishing operations cannot grip the boulder, and blasting would destroy the pilot hole. How can the obstruction be cleared without retrieving the entire string?
cases.reasoning_chain: Question: What if we could apply a huge force to break the boulder without adding a complex crushing mechanism to the reamer? Answer: We need high force (#10) but minimal added complexity (#36). Counterfactual: If the reamer could temporarily transform into a hammer, it might break the boulder. But adding a hammer mechanism is complex. What if we use the drill string itself as an energy conduit? The matrix gives #34 Discarding and recovering: could we discard a consumable element that does the work? #28 Mechanics substitution: replace mechanical crushing with a different field — perhaps an electro-hydraulic shockwave. #18 Mechanical vibration: use high-frequency torsional vibration transmitted down the string to fatigue the boulder. Combine: drop a frangible, heavy ring over the string that lands on the reamer body. Then apply high-amplitude, low-frequency axial vibration (percussion) from the surface rig. The ring acts as an anvil, concentrating impact energy into the boulder. Once the boulder fragments, the ring pieces fall into the chips and are recovered later.
cases.solution: A two-piece frangible steel collar is lowered on wireline over the drill string to rest on the stuck reamer. The raise-bore rig applies percussive axial blows (using the feed cylinders in rapid cycling mode) transmitted through the string to the collar-anvil. The boulder is shattered by concentrated impact stress. Collar fragments are later collected in the muck pile. Reaming resumed after 4 hours of percussion without tripping the string.

cases.problem: A nuclear power plant’s spent fuel pool requires continuous cooling. If the pump fails, the water temperature rises from 40°C to boiling in 8 hours. Installing a redundant, seismically qualified pump system costs $12M and requires a backup diesel generator. The plant seeks a passive cooling method that adds no moving parts and uses no external power, yet can reject 2 MW of decay heat for 72 hours post-shutdown.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The system must operate 100% reliably for 72 hours without power (improve Reliability #27). 2) Active redundant systems drastically increase Complexity of control (#37) due to sensors, switches, and logic. 3) Matrix (27,37) → {9, 36, 25, 40}. 4) #9 Preliminary Anti-Action: pre-install a thermal sink that absorbs heat before the accident. 5) #36 Phase Transitions: use the latent heat of water evaporation; the pool itself can boil off, carrying away heat. 6) #25 Self-Service: the decaying fuel generates heat that drives natural circulation and steam venting—no pumps needed. 7) #40 Composite Materials: line the pool with a porous wicking material that enhances evaporative cooling. 8) #1 Segmentation: divide the pool into multiple isolated cells so a single leak doesn't drain all water. 9) #10 Preliminary Action: pre-position a large, elevated water reservoir that can gravity-feed into the pool. 10) #11 Beforehand Cushioning: design the building structure to act as a giant condenser, capturing and recycling steam. 11) #12 Equipotentiality: eliminate height differences so natural circulation loops are symmetric and self-balancing. 12) #13 The Other Way Round: instead of cooling the pool, let it boil; the steam is vented and condensed passively. 13) #22 Blessing in Disguise: the boiling itself creates a pressure gradient that drives air-cooled heat exchangers. The final design is analogous to a heat-pipe: a sealed, segmented pool with a composite wick structure that evaporates water, condenses it on a finned ceiling, and returns it via gravity, all driven by decay heat.
cases.solution: A passive spent fuel pool cooling system based on a loop heat-pipe architecture. The pool is segmented into narrow, vertical channels lined with a sintered metal wick (#40, #1). Decay heat causes boiling (#36); steam rises and condenses on a large, air-cooled ceiling panel, and the condensate returns by gravity (#12). A pre-filled elevated tank (#10) provides makeup water. The system has zero moving parts and can operate indefinitely as long as water is present.

cases.problem: So, we've got this high-speed canning line for carbonated drinks, right? The filler nozzles have to plunge into each can, but the faster the line runs, the more foam we get because of turbulence. The foam makes the fill level inconsistent and slows us down because we have to let it settle. We're wasting product and time.
cases.reasoning_chain: The operators are frustrated. Every time they crank up the filler speed to meet quota, the cans foam over and they lose precious milliliters of soda. It's a classic trade-off: they need the speed, but the turbulence from the nozzle plunging into the liquid is the culprit. What if, instead of fighting the foam, they prevent the plunge? Instead of moving the nozzle down into the can, why not bring the can up to a stationary nozzle? That's #13 The Other Way Round — invert the action. But lifting each can precisely at high speed is tricky. So, let's also apply #28 Mechanics Substitution: replace the mechanical plunging with a magnetic field. The can has a steel rim; an electromagnetic collar around the stationary nozzle could momentarily levitate the can up to meet it. And for the liquid itself, #14 Spheroidality - Curvature suggests using a ball-shaped nozzle tip with a laminar-flow surface that lets the soda sheet down the can's inner wall without splashing. No plunge, no turbulence, no foam.
cases.solution: A stationary filling station with an electromagnetically actuated can lift and a spherical nozzle. Each can is momentarily levitated by a pulsed magnetic field to contact the nozzle, which has a curved, polished tip that guides a laminar sheet of soda down the can wall via the Coandă effect. Filling occurs without nozzle submersion, eliminating foam at line speeds exceeding 2000 cans per minute.

cases.problem: A customer writes: 'We love your forged titanium bicycle cranks, but the final machining to get the pedal thread concentricity within 10 microns is killing us. The forging is so hard and abrasive that our carbide inserts last maybe 15 minutes. We're spending more on tooling than on the titanium itself. Can't you make the forging closer to net shape so we don't have to cut so much?'
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The core issue: achieving high precision in a finished titanium forging causes severe tool wear (harmful factor) because of the material's hardness and the amount of stock to remove. 2) We want to improve Manufacturing precision (#29) but doing so worsens Object-generated harmful factors (#31) — the abrasive swarf and heat that destroy tools. 3) The contradiction matrix for (29,31) suggests principles {22, 34, 40, 35}. 4) Let's think analogously to how a beaver gnaws down a tree: it doesn't fight the wood's strength head-on; it uses its own sharp teeth and lets the wood chips fall away. But here the wood is titanium, and the 'teeth' are carbide. 5) Instead of fighting tool wear, apply #22 Blessing in disguise: use the heat generated by the cutting process. Titanium becomes more ductile at elevated temperature. What if we intentionally pre-heat the forging locally just in the thread boss area to 400°C before machining? The material softens, reducing cutting forces and tool wear. The very heat that normally destroys tools is now an advantage. 6) However, heating the whole crank is wasteful. Apply #34 Discarding and recovering: use a disposable, inductively-coupled copper 'cap' that fits over the boss. The cap heats up rapidly via induction, conducts heat into the titanium, and is then discarded or recycled after a single use. It's a cheap, short-life thermal intermediary. 7) But we still have the problem of the forging being too far from net shape. Apply #40 Composite materials: design the forging die with a sacrificial, net-shape insert made of a material that can be dissolved. Before forging, a precisely machined, water-soluble ceramic core is placed in the die to form the pedal thread's minor diameter and the complex internal geometry. After forging, the core is washed out with high-pressure water, leaving a near-net thread form that requires only a final chasing cut, removing 90% less material. 8) The combination: a soluble ceramic core for near-net forging, local induction pre-heating via a disposable copper cap to soften the material, and the resulting minimal machining cut that generates far less abrasive swarf. 9) Tool life increases from 15 minutes to over 4 hours. 10) The customer's machining cost per crank drops by 80%.
cases.solution: A near-net-shape forging process using a dissolvable alumina-based ceramic core to form the thread boss internal geometry. Prior to final thread chasing, a single-use copper induction cap heats the local boss area to 400°C, softening the titanium. A single-point threading tool then removes only 0.1 mm of material, with tool life extended to 4+ hours and swarf generation reduced by 90%.

cases.problem: We've been chasing a persistent issue on our new turboprop test stand. The exhaust gas temperature probes keep getting cooked. The probes sit right in the exhaust stream, which is a brutal 750°C, and while the thermocouples themselves are rated for it, the ceramic insulation and the stainless steel sheath start to degrade after only 200 hours. We keep losing signal and having to shut down for replacements. We need to measure that temperature accurately for engine tuning, but every time we try to protect the probe with a thicker sheath, the response time gets so slow the data is useless.
cases.reasoning_chain: Bob, the lead instrumentation tech, was frustrated. 'It's the same old story: we want to measure a high temperature (#17) accurately, but the harsh environment kills the sensor, so we beef it up, and then the accuracy (#28) goes out the window.' He drew a quick sketch. 'What if we don't try to make the whole probe tough? What if we just protect the tip? That's #3, Local quality. We could make the tip out of a super-alloy that can take the heat, but keep the rest of the probe thin for fast response.' He paused. 'But that still puts metal in the flow, and it'll erode. What if we change the state of the thing doing the measuring? #35, Parameter changes. Instead of a solid probe, we could use a pneumatic probe that sucks in a tiny sample of gas, cools it down by mixing, and then measures it. No more solid probe in the hot stream!' 'But that changes the measurement itself,' Sarah, the test engineer, countered. 'We need the true exhaust temperature. How about an intermediary, #24? We put a sacrificial, thin-walled radiation shield in front of the probe. The shield takes the brunt of the heat and erosion, and we measure the temperature behind it. We calibrate for the shield's effect, and we just replace the cheap shield every 100 hours.'
cases.solution: A hybrid temperature measurement system: a thin, fast-response thermocouple probe is placed behind a replaceable, thin-walled silicon carbide radiation shield. The shield blocks direct particle impingement and most radiative heat, allowing the probe to see a representative gas temperature. The shield is a cheap, short-living object (#27) replaced every 150 hours. A cooling air eductor on the probe body further drops the local temperature around the junction, and a dynamic compensation algorithm corrects for the thermal lag, restoring high measurement accuracy.

cases.problem: Aeroderivative gas turbines used for peak power generation must start rapidly (under 2 minutes) and reach full load to stabilize the grid. During a fast start, the compressor front casing heats up much faster than the internal rotor, causing a radial clearance pinch at the blade tips. This rub can damage blades and abradable coatings, eventually increasing steady-state tip clearance and reducing compressor efficiency by up to 3%. A slower start avoids the rub but fails the grid response requirement.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Goal: Minimize start-up time (#9) to meet grid demand. 2) Obstacle: Fast start causes a transient radial clearance pinch between the cold rotor and hot casing, leading to rubs. 3) The conventional fix is an active clearance control (ACC) system that pipes hot or cold air onto the casing to match its expansion to the rotor. This adds significant complexity (#36) with manifolds, valves, and control logic. 4) Look up contradiction matrix for (9,36): suggests principles {37, 15, 34, 28}. 5) Cross-domain analogy: Think of a bimetallic strip in a thermostat (principle #37, Thermal expansion). Can we make the casing itself respond to temperature differentially? 6) Apply #37: Construct the compressor casing from a high-expansion material (like an austenitic steel) and the rotor from a low-expansion material (like a martensitic steel or even a composite). The casing will naturally expand more than the rotor, opening the clearance during a fast heat-up. 7) But this would make the steady-state clearance too large. 8) Apply #15, Dynamics: Introduce a segmented outer casing with circumferential springs. As the casing gets hot, the segments expand radially outward against the springs, increasing clearance. As the rotor catches up in temperature, the springs pull the segments back in. 9) Apply #34, Discarding and recovering: Use a sacrificial, thick abradable coating on the casing that is designed to be rubbed once during the very first start-up. This 'bedding-in' rub creates a perfect, zero-clearance contour. The coating is then 'recovered' by a subsequent application of a thinner, smooth seal layer via a robotic spray process during a scheduled outage. 10) The primary solution uses #37 and #15: a passive, thermally-actuated segmented casing that opens clearance during the transient and closes it for steady-state efficiency, eliminating the need for an ACC system.
cases.solution: A passive thermal clearance control system. The compressor casing is circumferentially segmented, with each segment backed by shape-memory alloy (SMA) springs. During a fast start, the casing heats up rapidly; the SMA springs, sensing the temperature rise, relax and allow the segments to move radially outward (principle #37, #15). This increases tip clearance by 1.5 mm, preventing rub. As the rotor heats and expands over the next 3 minutes, the casing temperature stabilizes, and the springs recover their shape, pulling the segments back to a tight, efficient running clearance. The system requires no external piping, valves, or active control.

cases.problem: The transition duct of a land-based gas turbine combustor experiences severe low-cycle fatigue cracking due to cyclic thermal gradients. The duct must contain hot combustion gases at 1600°C while its outer wall is cooled by compressor discharge air at 450°C. Current design uses a thick-walled nickel alloy with thermal barrier coating (TBC), but the mismatch in thermal expansion between the hot inner surface and the cold outer surface generates large compressive stresses during start-up and tensile stresses during shutdown, leading to crack initiation after approximately 800 cycles. Increasing the wall thickness to reduce stress actually increases the thermal gradient and exacerbates the problem, while reducing thickness compromises structural integrity.
cases.reasoning_chain: This study addresses the inherent conflict between reducing thermo-mechanical stress (#11) and maintaining geometric stability (#12) in a combustor transition duct. The conventional approach of a thick, homogeneous wall is self-defeating, as the thickness amplifies the through-thickness thermal gradient, the root cause of stress. The contradiction matrix for (11,12) proposes principles {4, 40, 36, 35}. We first consider #4, Asymmetry. Instead of a symmetric wall, we design an asymmetric cross-section with a thin, hot inner skin and a thicker, cold outer structural shell, separated by a compliant layer. This decouples the thermal and structural functions. The inner skin, experiencing the highest temperature, is free to expand without generating large stresses because the compliant layer absorbs the differential strain. However, a simple gap would lose structural integrity. We therefore apply #40, Composite materials, to create a functionally graded material (FGM) transition layer that bonds the skin to the shell while providing a smooth gradient in thermal expansion coefficient, eliminating a sharp interface. To further manage the extreme heat flux, we integrate #36, Phase transitions. The compliant layer is designed as a porous matrix saturated with a low-melting-point metal (e.g., a sodium-potassium alloy). During operation, this metal melts and vaporizes, absorbing a substantial amount of heat as latent heat of fusion and vaporization. The vapor then diffuses towards the cooler shell and re-condenses, creating a highly effective, self-contained heat pipe that flattens the temperature profile across the wall. This phase-change mechanism actively reduces the thermal gradient, the primary source of stress, while the asymmetric composite structure maintains the duct's shape and pressure-bearing capability.
cases.solution: An asymmetric, actively cooled composite duct wall. It consists of: (1) a thin (1 mm) inner liner of oxide-dispersion-strengthened (ODS) alloy for creep resistance; (2) a 3 mm thick compliant, porous carbon-foam layer infiltrated with a NaK eutectic alloy, acting as a planar heat pipe via phase transition; (3) a 5 mm thick outer structural shell of a high-strength nickel alloy. The FGM bond coat between layers ensures mechanical integrity. The heat pipe effect reduces the through-thickness temperature difference from 1150°C to under 200°C, virtually eliminating thermal stress. Low-cycle fatigue life is projected to exceed 10,000 cycles.

cases.problem: A high-speed beverage bottling line must apply tamper-evident shrink sleeves to contoured bottles. The sleeve must conform tightly to complex bottle geometries without wrinkles or tears, but the current two-stage process (sleeve placement then heat shrinking) limits line speed to 300 bottles per minute. Increasing heat tunnel temperature to speed shrinking causes material scorching and higher reject rates.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The bottleneck is the heat tunnel dwell time. 2) Apply #10 Preliminary action: pre-shrink the sleeve partially onto a mandrel before bottle contact. 3) The sleeve is pre-formed to the bottle's contour in a separate, slower station, then transferred. 4) Apply #35 Parameter changes: switch from convective heat to targeted infrared radiation, which heats the polymer instantly without overheating the surrounding air. 5) The pre-shrunk sleeve requires only a final, rapid fixation on the line. 6) This decouples the shrinking physics from the main conveyor speed.
cases.solution: A rotary pre-shrinking module with IR emitters shapes sleeves onto mandrels offline. Shaped sleeves are then blown onto bottles at the main line, needing only a brief hot-air kiss for final conformity. Line speed increases to 600 bpm without scorching.

cases.problem: A hydroelectric dam's turbine runners suffer from cavitation pitting after only 3 years of operation, whereas the design life was 20 years. The repair requires draining the penstock and a 6-week shutdown costing $2M in lost generation. The plant manager demands a solution that prevents cavitation without reducing the turbine's peak efficiency of 94%.
cases.reasoning_chain: Q: We need to reduce cavitation pitting (#31), but don't we usually do that by slowing the turbine down? That would worsen speed (#9) and power output. A: Yes, that's the conventional trade-off. Q: What if we don't try to prevent the bubbles from forming, but instead prevent them from collapsing destructively? A: That's an inversion—Principle #13, The Other Way Round. Q: How can we stop a bubble from collapsing violently? A: Compressibility. If the bubble collapses into a compliant medium, the impact is absorbed. Q: So we inject something? Like air? A: Principle #28, Mechanics Substitution, suggests using a field instead of a substance. What if we use an acoustic field? Q: Ultrasonic transducers on the runner? A: Too complex. Principle #19, Periodic Action. What if we use the natural pressure pulsations? We can inject a small, periodic stream of air at the hub, creating a curtain of micro-bubbles that pass over the blades. These compressible bubbles cushion the collapsing cavitation bubbles, absorbing the shock without significantly reducing efficiency.
cases.solution: Inject a periodic, controlled stream of micro-air-bubbles from the turbine hub. The compressible air bubbles act as a cushion, absorbing the shock of collapsing cavitation bubbles and preventing pitting, without reducing turbine speed or efficiency.

cases.problem: Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants use molten salt for thermal storage. The salt freezes at 220°C. During a cloudy day, trace amounts of salt can solidify in the miles of piping, causing blockages that are nearly impossible to locate and clear without dismantling the entire pipe network.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Problem: Locating and clearing a frozen salt blockage in a CSP plant's piping network is extremely difficult. 2) We want to improve ease of repair (#34), but any complex internal inspection robot would greatly increase device complexity (#36). 3) Analogy: How do surgeons find and remove a blood clot in a complex vascular network without major surgery? They use a catheter with a guidewire, fluoroscopy, and a clot-dissolving drug. 4) Apply Principle #31 Porous Materials: What if the pipe has a 'porous' inner lining that holds a tracer fluid? 5) Principle #23 Feedback: We need to locate the blockage. Inject a harmless, heat-resistant acoustic reflector fluid that can only travel through clear pipes. 6) Listen with external acoustic sensors; the silence indicates the blockage location. 7) Principle #24 Intermediary: Now we need to clear it. Use a flexible, self-propelled 'pig' (pipeline inspection gauge). 8) But a solid pig can't pass a blockage. Principle #35 Parameter Changes: Make the pig's state changeable. 9) Principle #14 Spheroidality: The pig is a capsule with a curved, low-friction nose. 10) Principle #10 Preliminary Action: Pre-place heating elements along the pipe's exterior at known intervals. 11) Once the blockage is located via acoustic feedback, activate the nearest two heating elements to melt the salt plug locally. 12) Principle #4 Asymmetry: The pipe has a slightly asymmetric cross-section (a small, continuous groove at the bottom). 13) Principle #15 Dynamics: The pig has a spring-loaded fin that rides in this groove, keeping it oriented. The pig now moves through the melted zone, pushing the liquid salt out. The repair is minimally invasive, using feedback, intermediary agents, and dynamic local heating.
cases.solution: A self-propelled, dynamically adjustable pig with a low-friction spherical nose travels through a pipe with an asymmetric internal groove for guidance. External acoustic sensors locate blockages by detecting injected tracer fluid. Pre-installed external heating elements are then activated locally to melt the blockage, allowing the pig to clear it.

cases.problem: My new non-stick frying pan is fantastic for eggs, but the handle gets scorching hot on the stove. I love the lightweight feel, but I have to use an oven mitt every time I grab it, which is clumsy and dangerous when I'm trying to flip a pancake.
cases.reasoning_chain: The core issue is that a pan must withstand high cooking temperatures, but that same heat travels to the handle, creating a burn hazard. We want to maintain high temperature capacity of the pan body without the harmful heat on the handle. This is a classic contradiction between temperature (#17) and harmful factors (#30). Consulting the TRIZ matrix for (17,30) suggests principles like 40, 2, 33, and 19. Let's explore #40 Composite Materials: what if the handle isn't a single piece of metal but a layered structure? We could use a material with very low thermal conductivity bonded to a structural core. Then, #2 Taking Out: can we separate the 'hot' part from the 'hold' part? Imagine physically isolating the handle from the pan body with an insulating gap or intermediary. The solution emerges: a handle made from a composite of high-strength plastic resin reinforced with glass fibers, with a stainless steel core only at the very end for attachment, but the attachment point is a small cross-section with an air gap, so heat transfer is minimized. The result is a handle that stays cool even when the pan is blistering hot.
cases.solution: A frying pan handle constructed of a glass-reinforced phenolic resin composite, attached to the pan body via a small-diameter stainless steel rivet with an air gap between the handle flange and the pan wall, effectively breaking the thermal path.

cases.problem: A high-speed CNC milling operation requires a cutting tool that maintains extreme hardness at elevated temperatures, yet the tool must not be brittle and fracture under intermittent cutting forces.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Identify the core conflict: high-temperature hardness requires a stable, often ceramic-like phase, but this phase typically lacks toughness. 2) Apply #40 Composite Materials: design a tool with a functionally graded microstructure—a tough core and a hard, heat-resistant shell. 3) Apply #35 Parameter Changes: alter the grain size and binder content locally to transition from high toughness to high hardness. 4) Apply #15 Dynamics: incorporate a vibration-damping layer within the composite to absorb shock loads. 5) The tool body is sintered as a single piece with a cobalt-rich core for toughness and a titanium-carbide-rich outer layer for hardness. 6) The result is a tool that resists thermal softening and withstands interrupted cuts.
cases.solution: Functionally graded cemented carbide tool with a tough cobalt-rich core and a hard, heat-resistant titanium-carbide-rich outer layer, sintered as a single piece to eliminate bonding interfaces.

cases.problem: Achieving zero-defect surface finish in high-volume sheet metal stamping is challenged by the trade-off between tool longevity and part quality. A harder tool surface resists wear and maintains dimensional accuracy, but its increased brittleness makes it susceptible to micro-chipping, which then mars the workpiece surface.
cases.reasoning_chain: The fundamental conflict is that wear resistance (preventing tool material loss) is traditionally achieved through uniform high hardness, which introduces the harmful side effect of micro-chipping. A uniform bulk property cannot satisfy both extremes. We therefore decompose the tool's functional surface. Applying #3 Local Quality, we transition from a uniform hardened steel to a surface with a tailored microstructure: a hard, wear-resistant martensitic matrix containing dispersed, softer austenitic islands. These islands act as crack arrestors, preventing micro-chips from propagating. This is a specific #35 Parameter Changes at the microstructural level. However, some micro-chipping may still be inevitable over millions of cycles. We then apply #22 Blessing in Disguise: design the tool so that any inevitable micro-chip does not produce a raised burr that scratches the part, but instead creates a self-polishing crater. By controlling the stress state and grain orientation, a chip detaches cleanly and leaves a smooth, rounded depression that does not impair part finish. The tool thus exhibits high wear resistance with a self-healing, low-damage failure mode.
cases.solution: A stamping tool steel with a duplex microstructure of hard martensite and crack-arresting austenite islands, processed to induce a compressive residual stress state that promotes clean, non-damaging micro-chip formation.

cases.problem: A railway steel truss bridge from 1920 exhibits fatigue crack initiation at riveted gusset plate connections under modern heavy axle loads (32.5-tonne axle load, 120 MGT annual traffic). Replacement of all rivets with high-strength bolts is cost-prohibitive, but leaving them risks catastrophic failure.
cases.reasoning_chain: Q: What if we just ignore the fatigue cracks and keep operating? A: That would eventually lead to fracture of a tension member and possible collapse—unacceptable. Q: So we must strengthen the connections (improve #14). But if we drill out the rivets to replace them, we remove material and weaken the plates (worsen #23). Is there a way to strengthen without subtracting material? Q: The matrix for (14,23) gives us Intermediary, Homogeneity, and Mechanics Substitution. How could an intermediary help? A: Instead of removing rivets, we could introduce a load-transfer medium between the existing plates—perhaps injecting a high-strength adhesive or a low-viscosity metal filler into the gaps. Q: What about Homogeneity? The original steel is old mild steel; using a filler of similar composition might ensure thermal and mechanical compatibility. A: That suggests a metallic spray or a paste with similar properties. Q: Mechanics Substitution—can we replace the mechanical fastening with a chemical or thermal bond? A: Yes, we could use a combination of clamping with existing rivets and bonding with a structural epoxy, or even friction-based strengthening by inducing compressive stresses. Q: Putting it together, we can apply a controlled clamping force using shape memory alloy (SMA) washers under the rivet heads, then inject a steel-filled epoxy into the faying surfaces. The SMA provides active compression, the epoxy fills voids and transfers shear, and we've added strength without drilling a single hole.
cases.solution: Hybrid SMA-epoxy strengthening system: existing riveted gusset connections are strengthened in-situ by first cleaning faying surfaces via edge injection of solvent, then injecting a steel-powder-filled, low-viscosity structural epoxy (matching thermal expansion of base metal) to fill gaps and bond plates. Subsequently, pre-compressed nickel-titanium shape memory alloy washers are installed under accessible rivet heads; upon electrical heating to 80°C, they recover flat shape and exert 15 kN clamping force per rivet, shifting load transfer from shear to friction. Fatigue life increased by factor of 3.2 in full-scale tests, no rivet removal required, cost 30% of full bolted replacement.

cases.problem: A floating concrete pontoon bridge in a fjord experiences excessive differential settlement between adjacent pontoons during tidal changes (>2 m range), causing cracking in the roadway transition slabs. Articulated joints exist but lock up under high compressive loads from thermal expansion.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The pontoon bridge acts like a series of rigid bodies connected by joints; the effective length between joint centers (improve #4) must be minimized to accommodate tidal curvature, but shorter segments mean more joints, and each joint becomes a stress concentrator under thermal compression (worsen #11). 2) This is analogous to a segmented spinal column—how does nature handle flexibility under compression? Intervertebral discs allow multi-axis motion without locking. 3) TRIZ matrix for (4,11) suggests Segmentation, Another Dimension, The Other Way Round. 4) Apply #1 Segmentation: break the monolithic transition slab into multiple short, independent slabs that can rock individually. 5) Apply #17 Another Dimension: instead of a planar joint, create a three-dimensional articulation that permits rotation about two axes and limited translation—like a spherical bearing. 6) Apply #13 The Other Way Round: instead of a joint that resists compression, design a joint that uses compression to increase flexibility—like a pre-compressed elastomeric bearing that softens under vertical load. 7) Integrate all three: create a segmented roadway of precast panels, each supported on a central spherical rocker bearing that allows ±5° rotation, with inter-panel gaps filled by a pre-compressed, high-damping rubber extrusion that acts as a spring under tension but a flexible hinge under compression. 8) The rocker bearing is a concrete hemisphere grouted to the pontoon, with a PTFE-coated matching socket in the panel. 9) Under tidal tilt, panels rotate individually, preventing stress buildup. 10) Under thermal expansion, the rubber gap fillers compress and buckle in a controlled S-shape, absorbing displacement without locking. 11) Full-scale prototype tested to 1 million cycles; stress in transition slabs reduced from 12 MPa to 1.5 MPa.
cases.solution: Segmented rocker-panel roadway system: each 3 m long precast panel spans between pontoons on a central spherical concrete rocker bearing (radius 400 mm) with PTFE sliding surface, permitting ±6° rotation and ±50 mm lateral translation. Inter-panel gaps (30 mm) filled with pre-compressed, high-damping EPDM rubber blocks that stiffen in tension but buckle flexibly in compression, preventing joint lock-up. No cracking observed after 5 years of service with 2.4 m tidal range; maintenance reduced 80% compared to conventional articulated joints.

cases.problem: Customer complaint: 'Your new 'low-noise' gas pressure regulator is anything but. When I run my furnace at low fire, it makes a loud screeching whistle that wakes up the whole house. Your technician said it's because the valve is barely open and the gas velocity is sonic, but if you make the orifice bigger to slow it down, then it can't control the pressure at high flow. I just want it to be quiet at all settings without having to buy a second regulator.'
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The physical contradiction: The orifice must be small to regulate low flow, but small orifice creates high velocity and noise. 2) This is analogous to a camera aperture that needs to be both large and small for different light conditions. In cameras, we use a multi-blade iris that segments the opening. 3) TRIZ matrix for improving 'Noise' (#31) while not worsening 'Control complexity' (#37) suggests principles #1 (Segmentation), #17 (Another Dimension), #19 (Periodic Action). 4) Step 1: Apply #1 Segmentation — instead of a single variable orifice, use a cluster of many tiny orifices in parallel (like a showerhead). Each orifice stays below sonic velocity because total flow is divided. 5) Step 2: Apply #17 Another Dimension — arrange the orifices in a 3D stacked array, with each layer activated by a sliding port plate. At low flow, only one layer is exposed; at high flow, multiple layers. 6) Step 3: The port plate motion is binary (on/off per layer), so control is simple solenoid actuation, not a complex proportional valve. 7) Step 4: Apply #19 Periodic Action — instead of continuous flow through a fixed set of orifices, pulse the solenoids at high frequency. At low flow, one layer pulses at 50% duty cycle; effective flow is halved without reducing orifice size. 8) The pulsation frequency is above 20 kHz, so any residual noise is ultrasonic and inaudible. 9) Step 5: Cross-domain analogy confirmation: inkjet printers use segmented, pulsed orifices to control droplet size without moving parts. 10) Step 6: System integration — a microcontroller monitors downstream pressure and selects the number of active orifice layers and their pulse-width modulation duty cycle. 11) Result: flow range 100:1, noise below 25 dBA across the entire range, no complex mechanical feedback, and only two moving parts (the port plates).
cases.solution: A digital gas regulator using a 10-layer stacked array of micro-orifices, each layer enabled by a simple on/off solenoid port plate. Flow is controlled by the number of active layers and high-frequency pulse-width modulation of each layer. This eliminates high-velocity jet noise by distributing flow across many small, sub-sonic jets and shifting residual noise to ultrasonic frequencies.

cases.problem: Customer complaint: 'Every batch of your titanium alloy forgings we machine has a 0.2 mm deep alpha-case layer that destroys our carbide inserts in two passes. You told us to just machine it off, but the layer is uneven — some areas are 0.15 mm, others 0.35 mm. If we take a 0.4 mm roughing cut, we lose too much material and the part goes undersize on thin walls. We need a uniform, predictable alpha-case depth so we can program a single finishing pass.'
cases.reasoning_chain: Step 1: The alpha-case forms because oxygen diffuses into the titanium during forging at high temperature. Step 2: The depth varies because local temperature and gas flow are non-uniform in the furnace. Step 3: Improving manufacturing precision (#29) of the alpha-case depth worsens material loss (#23) because we must cut deeper to guarantee removal. Step 4: Matrix (29,23) → {10, 28, 32, 25}. But we need a deeper solution. Step 5: Analogy: In semiconductor doping, they use ion implantation to precisely control dopant depth. Can we apply a controlled diffusion barrier? Step 6: Principle #1 Segmentation: instead of one uniform coating, segment the surface into small areas and apply a local barrier. Step 7: Principle #14 Spheroidality: use spherical microcapsules of an oxygen-getter (like yttrium) in a binder. Step 8: Principle #40 Composite Materials: create a multi-layer anti-diffusion coating: a base layer that adheres to titanium, a middle layer of getter particles, and a top glass layer that seals. Step 9: Principle #10 Preliminary Action: apply this coating before heating; the getter particles react with incoming oxygen, preventing it from reaching the titanium. Step 10: The coating is designed to spall off uniformly on cooling, leaving a clean, predictable surface with no alpha-case.
cases.solution: A multi-layer anti-diffusion coating applied to titanium forgings before heating. The coating consists of a yttrium-stabilized zirconia base layer, a middle layer of yttrium microspheres (oxygen getter), and a borosilicate glass top seal. During forging, the getter captures oxygen, and the glass prevents diffusion. On cooling, the coating spalls off due to CTE mismatch, leaving zero alpha-case and eliminating the need for a roughing cut.

cases.problem: Abstract: In continuous casting of steel, the withdrawal rate is limited by the shell thickness at the mold exit; increasing speed reduces shell thickness, risking breakout. However, higher speed is desirable for productivity. Traditional solutions involve longer molds or more intensive spray cooling, both of which increase capital cost and can induce surface defects. This trade-off between productivity (speed) and reliability (object-generated harmful factors) is a classic contradiction. We seek a method to increase casting speed without compromising shell integrity.
cases.reasoning_chain: The contradiction is between Speed (#9) and Object-generated harmful factors (#31), where the harmful factor is the thin, weak shell. Matrix (9,31) yields {2, 24, 35, 21}. We explore an alternative path: the shell forms by solidification against the mold wall; its thickness grows with the square root of time. To increase speed, we need faster solidification, but simply extracting more heat through the mold wall causes surface cracks. Principle #35 Parameter Changes: change the physical state of the cooling medium. Instead of water, use a liquid metal coolant with higher thermal conductivity. But that introduces contamination. Principle #36 Phase Transitions: exploit the latent heat of a boiling coolant to extract heat more efficiently without extreme temperature gradients. However, the real insight comes from Principle #7 Nested Doll: place a smaller, consumable 'inner mold' inside the main mold that travels with the strand. This inner mold is a thin, high-conductivity tube that pre-solidifies a thicker shell before the strand exits the primary mold. The inner mold is made of a material that melts at a temperature just above the shell solidus, so it is continuously consumed and replaced, acting as a sacrificial heat sink. This decouples the solidification time from the mold length, allowing higher speeds.
cases.solution: A consumable inner mold system: a thin-walled copper alloy tube is fed into the mold alongside the strand, moving at the casting speed. It provides intense initial cooling, forming a thick shell rapidly. The tube is made of a low-melting-point copper alloy that melts at the hot face as the shell solidifies, and the molten alloy is continuously recirculated. This increases the effective solidification rate without lengthening the mold, enabling a 40% increase in casting speed while maintaining shell integrity.

cases.problem: An ultrasonic welding horn used to bond thin copper foils (0.1 mm) to a rigid busbar in battery pack assembly starts developing fatigue cracks near its mounting flange after only 20,000 cycles. The horn is made of titanium alloy and resonates at 20 kHz with a displacement amplitude of 40 μm at the tip. The crack initiates because the flange is a rigid constraint that creates a high-stress standing-wave node, yet the flange is necessary to hold the horn in the actuator.
cases.reasoning_chain: We have a classic fatigue problem: the horn must be rigidly held, but the holding point experiences peak vibrational stress. If we try to reduce stress by adding damping or a flexible coupling, the welding amplitude and efficiency drop. The contradiction is between reducing mechanical stress (#11) and not increasing device complexity (#36) with exotic materials or active control. From the TRIZ matrix for (11,36), we get principles {19, 1, 35, 15}. We pick #1 Segmentation and #15 Dynamics. Instead of one monolithic horn, we segment the horn into a driver section and a replaceable sonotrode tip, joined at a nodal point where stress is near zero. The flange is moved to the driver section's own nodal ring. Additionally, we make the joint between sections slightly adjustable (Dynamics) so the horn length can be fine-tuned to maintain exact resonance as the tip wears. This way, the high-stress region is confined to the cheap, replaceable tip, while the main body operates at low stress, and the flange is located at a natural zero-displacement point.
cases.solution: Segment the horn into a titanium driver with an integral flange at its nodal ring, and a separate, threaded sonotrode tip. The joint is precisely at the anti-nodal displacement peak but at a stress minimum. The tip is a consumable item, replaced every 50,000 cycles. An adjustable locking ring allows fine-tuning the acoustic length to compensate for tip erosion, maintaining resonance without redesigning the entire horn.

cases.problem: A continuous galvanizing line applies a zinc coating to steel strip moving at 180 m/min. The strip exits a molten zinc bath and passes between two air knives that blow off excess zinc to control coating thickness. The air knives use compressed air at 6 bar. The high-velocity air jet atomizes the liquid zinc at the strip edges, creating zinc dust (dross) that contaminates the air knives, clogs nozzles, and deposits on the strip, causing surface defects. Lowering the air pressure reduces dross but fails to achieve the target 20 μm coating uniformity.
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) Concrete: air knife jet atomizes edge zinc → dross. Reducing pressure reduces dross but also reduces wiping force, so coating is too thick/non-uniform. 2) Improving: reduce material loss (#23) — the zinc that becomes dross. 3) Worsening: the wiping force (#10) must remain high enough to control coating thickness. 4) Matrix (23,10) → {14, 29, 10, 39}. 5) Select #14 Spheroidality - Curvature: change the shape of the interaction zone. Instead of a flat air jet hitting a flat strip edge, curve the strip edge slightly downward (by edge rolls) so the liquid zinc flows away from the air knife's direct blast. 6) Select #25 Self-service: use the zinc itself to protect the edge. The curved edge creates a small meniscus of zinc that is thicker and more stable, resisting atomization. 7) Analogy: a river bank that curves gently inward creates a stable, slower-flowing boundary layer that prevents erosion. 8) The air knife still delivers high force on the main strip surface for thickness control, but at the very edge, the curved geometry and self-formed zinc bead prevent the high-velocity air from directly impinging on a thin, unstable liquid sheet. 9) Dross formation drops significantly because atomization requires thin liquid sheets; the curved edge maintains a thicker, stable liquid bead. 10) Coating uniformity is maintained because the edge bead is trimmed off later in the process anyway.
cases.solution: Install a pair of small forming rolls just above the air knives that impart a slight downward curvature (radius ~50 mm) to the strip edges. This causes the liquid zinc at the edges to form a stable, thick meniscus due to surface tension. The air knife jets impinge on the flat central region for precise thickness control, while the curved edges deflect the air smoothly and retain a protective zinc bead that resists atomization. The edge bead is subsequently trimmed and recycled, resulting in a dross-free, uniform coating.

cases.problem: In selective laser melting (SLM) of Inconel 718, the rapid solidification and repeated thermal cycling create high residual tensile stresses in the as-built part, often exceeding 800 MPa and causing distortion or cracking during the build. A heated build plate (up to 200°C) is insufficient to relieve these stresses in situ. A full post-build stress-relief heat treatment is required, but this adds 4-6 hours to the production cycle and cannot prevent build failures. In-situ stress relaxation without slowing the build rate remains an open challenge.
cases.reasoning_chain: This study addresses the trade-off between residual stress mitigation (improving parameter #11) and process productivity (#39) in powder-bed fusion. Traditional thermal methods are either too slow (in-situ annealing) or ineffective (heated plate). We consult the contradiction matrix for (11,39) and retrieve principles {35, 39, 19, 10}. We select #35 Parameter Changes and #18 Mechanical Vibration. Instead of altering the thermal state, we alter the mechanical state during solidification. By applying high-frequency, low-amplitude vibration to the build plate during the laser scan, we induce micro-plastic deformation in the just-solidified metal while it is still at elevated temperature. This dynamic stress relaxation mechanism (akin to vibratory stress relief but localized in time and space) reduces the accumulation of tensile residual stress without requiring a dwell time or reduced scan speed. The vibration parameters (frequency, amplitude) are tuned to the part geometry and scan strategy, representing a critical parameter change from purely thermal to thermo-mechanical processing. The build rate is maintained because the laser scan speed is unchanged; the vibration is superimposed and continuous.
cases.solution: Integrate a piezoelectric actuator array beneath the build plate to deliver 20 kHz ultrasonic vibrations with 5 μm amplitude during the SLM process. The vibrations are synchronized with the laser scanning path to induce localized cyclic plasticity in the heat-affected zone, reducing tensile residual stress by 60% to under 300 MPa in as-built Inconel 718. This eliminates build failures and reduces post-processing time by 80%, with no reduction in laser scan speed.

cases.problem: So here's the thing: we're building this massive grid-scale battery storage system, right? And we want to pack as many cells as possible into a shipping container to hit our 5 MWh target. But our engineers are screaming about thermal runaway. If one cell goes, the heat spreads to neighbors in seconds, and you get a chain reaction. We need high energy density, but we can't have the whole thing blowing up. How do we stop fire propagation without spacing the cells apart and killing our density?
cases.reasoning_chain: We want to minimize the volume occupied by the battery system (improve #8) by packing cells tightly. But tight packing means a thermal runaway event in one cell rapidly propagates heat and fire to adjacent cells, creating a catastrophic hazard (worsen #30). The classical solution is to increase cell spacing or add heavy firewalls, but that sacrifices volume. The contradiction matrix for (8,30) suggests principles {22, 18, 39, 16}. Let's explore #22 'Blessing in Disguise'—instead of fighting the heat, can we use it? In a thermal runaway, the cell vents hot gases and particles. We could design the module so that these vented products are channeled to activate a suppression mechanism. Next, #31 'Porous Materials': what if we fill the interstitial space between cells with a highly porous, thermally insulating but gas-permeable foam? This foam can absorb the initial blast, filter particles, and allow controlled venting. Then #39 'Inert Atmosphere': we can pre-fill the sealed module with an inert gas like nitrogen. When a cell vents, the inert atmosphere prevents external combustion. The porous foam also acts as a flame arrestor. The result is a cell-to-cell spacing of less than 2 mm, achieving high energy density, while the combination of vent channeling, porous flame arrestor, and inert atmosphere prevents cascading thermal runaway.
cases.solution: Ultra-dense battery module with integrated safety: cells are packed with only 1.5 mm gaps filled with a ceramic-fiber-reinforced aerogel felt (porosity >90%). The module is hermetically sealed and backfilled with nitrogen. Cell vent ports are aligned to a common duct leading to a burst disc, directing hot ejecta away from neighboring cells. In tests, a single-cell thermal runaway did not propagate to adjacent cells.

cases.problem: BUG REPORT: Battery Management System (BMS) voltage sensing accuracy drifts by up to ±15 mV after 6 months of vehicle operation. Root cause traced to oxidation of tin-plated contacts at the cell terminal sense harness due to vibration-induced fretting and humidity ingress. Improving measurement accuracy requires gold-plated contacts and hermetic sealing, but that makes the wiring harness rigid and impossible to service without destroying the connector.
cases.reasoning_chain: Q: What if we accept the fretting corrosion but detect it instead of preventing it? A: That could work—we could use a sacrificial sensing element that corrodes predictably and indicates when accuracy is lost. But the requirement is to maintain accuracy, not just detect failure. Q: What if we make the gold contact reusable? A: The problem is the rigid connection. What if we separate the electrical and mechanical functions? Q: How about using an intermediary? Apply #24 Intermediary: insert a thin, replaceable conductive polymer interposer between the cell terminal and the rigid harness connector. The interposer conforms to surface irregularities, blocks humidity, and can be easily swapped during service. But polymer conductivity may drift. Q: What if we replace the physical contact entirely? Apply #28 Mechanics Substitution: use a capacitive or inductive coupling for voltage sensing, eliminating galvanic contact. This removes the fretting problem entirely but may introduce noise. Q: Can we combine these? Use a non-contact sensor for primary measurement and a simple, disposable contact for calibration. Apply #32 Color Changes: embed a humidity indicator in the connector housing that changes color when the seal is compromised, prompting preemptive interposer replacement before accuracy drifts. This decouples accuracy from repairability: the accurate part is contactless, the serviceable part is a cheap, replaceable seal with visual health indicator.
cases.solution: Hybrid BMS sensing: primary voltage sensing via a high-impedance capacitive pickup coil integrated into the cell holder, providing ±2 mV accuracy. A secondary, low-cost spring-contact interposer made of carbon-nanotube-impregnated silicone is used only for initial factory calibration and field diagnostics. The connector housing includes a cobalt-chloride humidity indicator spot that turns from blue to pink, signaling interposer replacement. The capacitive sensor requires no physical contact and is immune to fretting.

cases.problem: Customer Complaint: 'I bought your electric SUV for camping, and the battery range drops 40% in winter because the resistive cabin heater eats all the power. Your heat pump works great above freezing, but below -10°C it's useless. I need full winter range without freezing to death!'
cases.reasoning_chain: 1) The goal is to maintain cabin comfort at low ambient temperatures (improve #17) without excessive battery drain (worsen #19). 2) The direct contradiction: resistive heating is reliable at any temperature but consumes 1 kWh of electricity per kWh of heat (COP=1). A heat pump has COP>2 but loses capacity below -10°C. 3) Matrix lookup (17,19) → {1, 28, 35, 36}. 4) Cross-domain analogy: In cryogenic engineering, a Claude cycle uses a phase-change working fluid and a recuperative heat exchanger to achieve very low temperatures efficiently. 5) Apply #36 Phase Transitions: Use a refrigerant with a very low boiling point (e.g., R-744, CO2) that can extract heat from -20°C air by evaporating, then release it at high temperature via a transcritical cycle. 6) Apply #20 Continuity of Useful Action: The heat pump generates waste cold air after extracting heat; instead of dumping it, route it through a thermal storage module (e.g., a compact phase-change material heat exchanger) to pre-cool the battery or power electronics, recovering exergy. 7) Apply #15 Dynamics: The compressor and expansion valve are digitally controlled to operate in a 'boost' mode (transcritical for very low ambient) or a standard subcritical mode for milder temperatures, optimizing COP across the full range. 8) Apply #5 Merging: Integrate the cabin heat exchanger with the battery thermal management loop. When the battery needs cooling after fast charging, the heat pump can move that waste heat to the cabin, reducing the total energy spent. 9) The system uses a small 500 W resistive heater only for initial defrost and as a backup below -25°C. 10) Result: The system achieves a seasonal average COP of 2.8, reducing winter range loss to less than 15%.
cases.solution: Integrated transcritical CO2 heat pump with multi-mode operation and thermal storage. The system uses a CO2 refrigerant loop that operates transcritical below -10°C, providing 2.5 kW of heat at -20°C with COP>1.8. A 2 kWh PCM thermal buffer (paraffin, 15°C phase change) stores waste cold for electronics cooling and waste heat from the battery for cabin preheating. A unified controller merges cabin and battery thermal demands, minimizing total energy expenditure.

cases.problem: Smartwatch display brightness is capped at 1000 nits in direct sunlight due to a thermal throttle that kicks in after 3 minutes. We need 2000 nits sustained for outdoor readability, but the AMOLED panel temperature rises by 12°C per 100 nits above 1000, and we cannot exceed a 45°C skin-contact limit.
cases.reasoning_chain: Q: We need more light output (Illumination intensity #18) but that directly heats the display (Temperature #17). What does the matrix say? A: (18,17) → {32, 35, 19, 13}. Q: Let's explore #32 Color Changes. Could we change the color of something to reduce heat? A: What if the watch face background turns white in sunlight? That reflects ambient light instead of absorbing it, but doesn't help the panel's self-heating. Q: Counterfactual — what if the display itself became transparent to let sunlight through, like a transflective LCD? A: That's #13 The Other Way Round — instead of fighting sunlight with more emitted light, use sunlight as part of the illumination. Q: But we still need high brightness for content. How about #19 Periodic Action? A: What if we pulse the brightness at 240 Hz, invisible to the eye, reducing average power? The peak can be 2000 nits but duty cycle 50%, halving the heat generation. Q: Could we combine #32 and #13? A: Use a photochromic layer (#32) that darkens in sunlight, so the perceived contrast is maintained even at 1000 nits. The watch doesn't need to emit 2000 nits if the ambient light is blocked from washing out the screen. Q: So the solution is to let the sun do the work for the background and block it for the pixels? A: Exactly — a micro-louver film (#13) that acts like tiny venetian blinds, directing ambient light onto a reflective pixel backplane while blocking it from the emissive OLED layer, combined with a periodic brightness boost synchronized with the ambient light sensor.
cases.solution: A hybrid display stack: a photochromic filter layer that darkens to 50% transmission under >50k lux, maintaining black levels. A micro-louver film directs incoming sunlight to a reflective layer behind the OLED, boosting ambient contrast. The OLED driver uses pulse-width modulation at 240 Hz with a 60% duty cycle at 2000 nits peak, achieving an average heat load equivalent to 1200 nits continuous. Skin temperature stays at 43°C after 30 minutes.

cases.problem: Rail neutral temperature (RNT) of continuous welded rail (CWR) on a 120 km desert line varies from 18°C to 62°C ambient. We record 14 buckles per summer despite re-stressing every 3 years. The current manual RNT measurement using a rail stress module takes 20 minutes per point and covers only 1% of the line annually.
cases.reasoning_chain: Q: What if we could measure stress in every meter of rail continuously? A: That would require thousands of sensors, which is impractical. Q: But what if the rail itself could report its stress state? A: That sounds like a color change principle. Q: Could we paint the rail web with a strain-sensitive coating that changes hue? A: Yes, but how would we read it automatically? Q: What if a camera on a hi-rail vehicle images the rail at 80 km/h and a neural network interprets the color pattern? A: That replaces manual point measurements with a continuous optical feedback loop. Q: But paint degrades under UV and abrasion. Could we use the natural oxide layer? A: That's parameter change. By controlling the rail surface finish during grinding, we can create a micro-texture that exhibits stress-dependent light polarization. Q: Then a polarized camera system on a track inspection vehicle reads the RNT at line speed. A: Exactly — we trade off the precision of a single-point mechanical gauge for a continuous, non-contact optical measurement that covers the whole line daily.
cases.solution: A rail stress monitoring system using a controlled micro-textured rail surface (created during preventive grinding) that exhibits stress-induced birefringence. A train-mounted polarized high-speed camera captures the rail image at 100 km/h; onboard processing reconstructs RNT to ±1.5°C accuracy every 10 cm. Daily coverage identifies buckle precursors, reducing buckles by 80% without any wayside instrumentation.
